fields.question_text: A knot or sharp radius bend of a towline reduces rope strength by:
fields.option_d: no appreciable reduction in strength
fields.explanation: "A knot in the towline reduces its strength by up to 50 percent, and causes a high spot in the rope that is more susceptible to wear. Pay particular attention to the ring area to which the glider attaches because this is also a high-wear area." Glider Flying Handbook (2013), page 5-5

fields.question_text: The primary function of the rudder is to:
fields.option_a: turn the glider
fields.option_b: counteract aileron drag
fields.option_c: assist in recovery from stalls and spins
fields.option_d: provide extra propulsion at the beginning of the aerotow
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook, page 3-12

When you roll into a turn, the aileron on the inside of the turn is raised and the aileron on the outside of the turn is lowered. The lowered aileron on the outside increases the angle of attack and produces more lift for that wing. Since induced drag is a by-product of lift, the outside wing also produces more drag than the inside wing. This causes a yawing tendency toward the outside of the turn called adverse yaw. Coordinated use of rudder and aileron corrects for adverse yaw and aileron drag.

fields.question_text: What is the indicated never-exceed speed of the ASK-21 when flying at 20,000 feet MSL?
fields.option_a: 151 knots
fields.option_b: 144 knots
fields.option_c: 132 knots
fields.option_d: 121 knots
fields.explanation: VNE is determined by flight testing and depends on the structural characteristics of an aircraft under varying aerodynamic conditions.  For most gliders, the never-exceed speed drops off significantly above 5000 MSL, but the actual limits are as specified in the Pilot Operating Handbook or equivalent document.  These limits are also included for convenience in the SSC cockpit cards.

fields.question_text: A small wisp of a cloud may indicate the start of a growing:
fields.option_a: cumulus cloud
fields.option_b: stratus cloud
fields.option_c: squall line
fields.option_d: cirrus cloud
fields.explanation: Glider Flying Handbook, page 9-7, figure 9-8, "Life cycle of a typical thermal with cumulus cloud."

fields.question_text: The cloud type that produces hazards such as hail, heavy rain, and violent updrafts is the
fields.option_a: nimbostratus
fields.option_b: cumulus
fields.option_c: cumulonimbus
fields.option_d: stratonimbus
fields.explanation: Glider Flying Handbook , page G-2 definition for Cumulonimbus (CB)

fields.question_text: The best course of action for a sailplane that encounters a cumulonimbus cloud is to
fields.option_a: circle downwind of the cloud
fields.option_b: remain 1000 feet below the base of the cloud
fields.option_c: circle around the edges of the cloud
fields.option_d: leave the area

fields.question_text: Unless supplemental oxygen is used, some deterioration of physical and mental performance occurs after prolonged flight at
fields.explanation:
AIM 8-1-2 Hypoxia

2. Although a deterioration in night vision occurs at a cabin pressure altitude as low as 5,000 feet, other significant effects of altitude hypoxia usually do not occur in the normal healthy pilot below 12,000 feet. From 12,000 to 15,000 feet of altitude, judgment, memory, alertness, coordination and ability to make calculations are impaired, and headache, drowsiness, dizziness and either a sense of well-being (euphoria) or belligerence occur. The effects appear following increasingly shorter periods of exposure to increasing altitude. In fact, pilot performance can seriously deteriorate within 15 minutes at 15,000 feet.

Glider Flying Handbook, page 10-12

At around 20,000 feet MSL pilots might have only 10 minutes of “useful consciousness.” By 30,000 feet MSL, the timeframe for “useful consciousness” decreases to one minute or less.

fields.question_text: The greatest danger when flying at high altitudes is a condition known as
fields.option_a: dehydration
fields.option_b: asphyxiation
fields.option_c: hyperventilation
fields.option_d: hypoxia
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook, page 1-10

High-altitude flying, which glider pilots encounter when mountain wave soaring or thermal soaring at high elevations, can place you in danger of becoming hypoxic. Oxygen starvation causes the brain and other vital organs to become impaired.

fields.question_text: Rapid breathing, or hyperventilation, results from an inadequate supply of:
fields.option_a: carbon dioxide
fields.option_b: carbon monoxide
fields.option_c: oxygen
fields.option_d: nitrogen
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook, page 1-12

Hyperventilation occurs when you are experiencing emotional stress, fright, or pain, and your breathing rate and depth increase although the carbon dioxide is already at a reduced level in the blood. The result is an excessive loss of carbon dioxide from your body, which can lead to unconsciousness due to the respiratory system’s overriding mechanism to regain control of breathing.

fields.question_text: The safest course of action for the pilot in command who is suffering from motion sickness is to:
fields.option_a: drink some water
fields.option_b: close the air vents
fields.option_c: take a dramamine tablet
fields.option_d: land the glider, when practicable
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook, page 1-14

It is important to remember that experiencing airsickness is no reflection on your ability as a pilot. Let your flight instructor know if you are prone to motion sickness since there are techniques that can be used to overcome this problem. For example, you may want to avoid lessons in turbulent conditions until you are more comfortable in the glider, or start with shorter flights and graduate to longer instruction periods. If you experience symptoms of motion sickness during a lesson, you can alleviate some of the discomfort by opening fresh air vents or by focusing on objects outside the glider. Although medication like Dramamine can prevent airsickness in passengers, it is not recommended while you are flying since it can cause drowsiness.

Although opening air vents is suitable for more minor cases of motion sickness, answer D is the best choice for solo flight.

fields.question_text: Pilots suffering from chronic or acute fatigue just prior to a planned flight should:
fields.option_a: drink plenty of coffee
fields.option_b: eat a chocolate bar
fields.option_c: bring a can of redbull along for the flight
fields.option_d: stay on the ground
fields.explanation:
AIM 8-1-1

3. Chronic fatigue occurs when there is not enough time for full recovery between episodes of acute fatigue. Performance continues to fall off, and judgment becomes impaired so that unwarranted risks may be taken. Recovery from chronic fatigue requires a prolonged period of rest.

fields.question_text: The best way to prevent both dehydration and heat stroke is to carry and use
fields.option_a: salt tablets
fields.option_b: coffee
fields.option_c: soft drinks
fields.option_d: water
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook, page 6-4

The glider pilot should carry water on every flight to prevent dehydration. The effects of dehydration on a pilot’s performance are subtle, but can be dangerous and are especially a factor in warmer climates.

fields.question_text: Any time the pilot is flying above 10,000 feet MSL (and 1,200 AGL) the visibility required for VFR flight is:
fields.option_a: 5 nautical miles
fields.option_b: 1 statute miles
fields.option_c: 3 statute mile
fields.option_d: 5 statute miles
fields.explanation:
§ 91.155   Basic VFR weather minimums.

(a) Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this section and §91.157, no person may operate an aircraft under VFR when the flight visibility is less, or at a distance from clouds that is less, than that prescribed for the corresponding altitude and class of airspace in the following table:

Airspace Flight visibility Distance from clouds
Class E:    
Less than 10,000 feet MSL 3 statute miles 500 feet below.
1,000 feet above.
2,000 feet horizontal
At or above 10,000 feet MSL 5 statute miles 1,000 feet below.
1,000 feet above.
1 statute mile horizontal.

fields.question_text: How many feet in altitude will a sailplane lose in 15 nautical miles if its L/D ratio is 22:1
fields.explanation:
Use the following equation: 

 Number of feet in a nautical mile x number of nautical miles

glide ratio

(6080 * 15)
22		= 4145

fields.question_text: Proper clothing is an important consideration
fields.option_a: in the summer
fields.option_b: in the winter
fields.option_c: for long flights
fields.option_d: for all flights
fields.explanation: Whether it's blowing snow, or it's scorching heat, a pilot really needs to consider his clothing choices before a flight.

fields.question_text: If the towplane should lose power during the takeoff roll, what action should be taken by the sailplane pilot?
fields.option_a: an immediate release and turn to the left
fields.option_b: an immediate release and straight ahead stop, avoiding the towplane to the left, if necessary
fields.option_c: an immediate release and turn to the right
fields.option_d: an immediate release and straight ahead stop, avoiding the towplane to the right, if necessary
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook pages 7-4 and 7-5

Situation 1. If the towrope breaks or is inadvertently released prior to the towplane’s liftoff, the standard procedure is for the towplane to continue the takeoff and clear the runway, or abort the takeoff and remain on the left side of the runway. If the towplane loses power during the takeoff, the towpilot should maneuver the towplane to the left side of the runway. If the glider is still on the runway, the glider pilot should pull the release, decelerate using the wheel brake, and be prepared to maneuver to the right side of the runway.

fields.question_text: The purpose of clearing turns is to:
fields.option_a: check for thermals
fields.option_b: check for traffic
fields.option_c: reduce airspeed
fields.option_d: check flight controls
fields.explanation: A clearing turn is done to check for traffic before maneuvers such as minimum controllable airspeed, stalls and spins.

fields.question_text: When a side slip is performed during a crosswind landing, the ground track should be:
fields.option_a: sideways, in the direction of the lowered wing
fields.option_b: in the opposite direction of the crosswind
fields.option_c: a gentle curve towards the runway centerline
fields.option_d: straight and parallel to the runway and the sailplane's longitudinal axis
fields.explanation: The point of the side slip is to counteract any crosswind, while maintaining the runway's extended centerline.  The difference between a side slip and crabbing is that the side slip counteracts the crosswind while maintaining the aircraft's longitudinal axis along the ground track.  Crabbing is when the aircraft's nose is pointed upwind to maintain ground track.

fields.question_text: If too much altitude is lost on the base leg and it looks like you will not make it to the runway:
fields.option_a: Close the spoilers, fly no slower than best L/D corrected for wind until time to flare
fields.option_b: Head directly for a short final, look for safest spots to make a controlled touchdown short of the runway
fields.option_c: Be prepared to intentionally ground-loop the glider after a controlled touchdown if necessary to avoid hitting objects head-on
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation: If you have misjudged your pattern and will not make the runway, you may be out of good choices.  Slowing down below best L/D speed does not stretch the glide.  Attempting a low turn to final risks catching a wingtip or skidding into a turn resulting in a spin.  Your choice of touchdown spots should prioritize minimizing the likelihood of injury.

fields.question_text: Practice manuevers should be entered at an altitude no lower than:
fields.option_a: 1,500 feet AGL
fields.option_b: As required to complete by 1,500 feet AGL
fields.option_c: 1,000 feet AGL
fields.option_d: 2,200 feet MSL
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-27, 

"Intentional stalls should be performed so the maneuver is completed by 1,500 feet above the ground for recovery and return to normal, wings-level flight. "

fields.question_text: Directional control is maintained after a crosswind touchdown by applying
fields.option_a: aileron into the wind
fields.option_b: aileron away from the wind
fields.option_c: rudder into the wind
fields.option_d: whatever is necessary to track the centerline - could be rudder away from the wind to control weather-vaning, could be aileron into the wind to hold wings level, could be aileron away from the wind to deliberately lower the downwind wing to the runway to keep the upwind wing above runway lights if unable to track the centerline
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-36 through 7-38

The GFH references focus on final approach and the flare.  AFTER touchdown in a crosswind, which is the question, use whatever controls are necessary.  Aileron into the wind may be needed to keep a crosswind from blowing the upwind wing up, while attempting to maintain wings level.  Rudder away from the wind may be required to keep the glider from weather-vaning into the wind. If the glider is still weather-vaning, a deliberate but gentle aileron input away from the wind to fly the downwind wing to the runway may help straighten the glider out and keep the upwind wing above runway edge lights if departing the centerline (while applying brakes).

fields.question_text: The sailplane's groundspeed during a downwind landing as compared to a normal upwind landing is normally:
fields.option_a: higher
fields.option_b: lower
fields.option_c: about the same
fields.option_d: twice as high
fields.explanation: Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-38. "The tailwind increases the touchdown groundspeed and lengthens the landing roll. The increased distance for landing can be determined by dividing the actual touchdown speed by the normal touchdown speed and squaring the result. "

fields.question_text: When a loss of lift occurs immediately after starting the turn in a thermal, it indicates
fields.option_a: a turn in the wrong direction
fields.option_b: a late turn
fields.option_c: an early turn
fields.option_d: a proper turn

fields.question_text: The pilot makes initial contact with a thermal and turns to the right. Between 90 degrees and 180 degrees of rotation from the initial contact, the variometer drops from 3 knots lift to 0 knots. This decrease in lift indicates:
fields.option_a: a turn in the wrong direction
fields.option_b: a late turn
fields.option_c: an early turn
fields.option_d: a proper turn
fields.explanation: GFH, page 10-6. "Soaring pilots’ opinions differ regarding how long to wait after encountering lift and before rolling into the thermal. Some pilots advocate flying straight until the lift has peaked. Then, they start turning, hopefully back into stronger lift. It is imperative not to wait too long after the first indication that the thermal is decreasing for this maneuver. Other pilots favor rolling into the thermal before lift peaks, thus avoiding the possibility of losing the thermal by waiting too long. Turning into the lift too quickly will cause the glider to fly back out into sink. There is no one right way; the choice depends on personal preference and the conditions on a given day. Timing is everything and practice is key."

fields.question_text: When searching for a thermal the recommended speed to maintain is
fields.option_a: manuevering speed
fields.option_b: Best L/D speed for wind and lift condition
fields.option_c: minimum control speed
fields.option_d: minimum sink speed

fields.question_text: The best airspeed to use while thermalling is
fields.option_a: manuevering speed
fields.option_b: Best L/D speed
fields.option_c: minimum control speed
fields.option_d: minimum sink speed, considering the angle of bank being used for the turn to stay in the thermal
fields.explanation: The best airspeed to use while thermalling is a compromise between minimizing turn radius and minimizing sink rate.  See Glider Flying Handbook pages 3-12 and 10-6 through 10-11.  Minimum sink speed at 40 degrees of bank (common in a thermal) is faster than minimum sink speed in level flight in calm air.  Maneuvering speed would be way too fast.  Best L/D speed is normally too fast.  Minimum control speed is too slow, even if thermalling straight and level in a cloud street.

fields.question_text: The crest of mountain waves may be marked by
fields.option_a: cumulus clouds
fields.option_b: cirrus clouds
fields.option_c: lenticular clouds
fields.option_d: rotor clouds

fields.question_text: when flying near mountain wave, the turbulent area beneath the wave may be marked by
fields.option_a: cumulus clouds
fields.option_b: cirrus clouds
fields.option_c: lenticular clouds
fields.option_d: rotor clouds
fields.explanation:
Cumulus clouds are cauliflower-shaped.  They are caused by lifting from below.  They are not usually associated with wave activity. 

Cirrus clouds (actually cirro-stratus clouds) are generally indications of a warm frontal passage that is approaching in the next few days. 

Lenticular clouds are the smooth, almond shaped clouds that are located in the smooth part of the wave lift.  Their presence does not indicate turbulence where the lenticular clouds are. 

Rotor clouds are the gnarly clouds that reside under the lenticular clouds.  They are the ones that indicate turbulence.

fields.question_text: For mountain wave to form, the minimum wind speed across the mountain tops should be:
fields.option_a: 10 mph
fields.option_b: 15-20 knots
fields.option_c: only when 30 mph or more
fields.option_d: only when 40 mph or more
fields.explanation:
GFH 2013, page 9-18: "The weather requirements for wave soaring include sufficient wind and a proper stability profile. Wind speed should be at least 15 to 20 knots at mountaintop level with increasing winds above."

(updated ref to 2013 GFH page number; previous version had 15 mph for both answers A and B, neither of which matched the GFH)

fields.question_text: A Student Pilot Certificate (that was issued after 1 April 2016) ...
fields.option_a: is valid for 24 calendar months if the student is under 40 years of age, 60 calendar months if the student is over 40 years old
fields.option_b: is valid for 36 calendar months
fields.option_c: is valid for 60 calendar months
fields.option_d: has no expiration date.
fields.explanation:
If you've read through the FARs, you'll see the reg:

§ 61.19   Duration of pilot and instructor certificates.

(b) Student pilot certificate.

(3) For student pilots seeking a glider rating, balloon rating, or a sport pilot certificate, the student pilot certificate does not expire until 60 calendar months after the month of the date issued, regardless of the person's age.

However, after 1 April 2016, the method for handing out student pilot certificates changed.  They used to be flimsy paper certificates, and are now credit card sized certificates similar to the private and commercial pilot certificates. So technically speaking, C is correct if the certificate was issued before 1 April 2016.  If it was issued after 1 April 2016, it has no expiration date.

fields.question_text: What is the AIM and where can it be found?
fields.option_a: The Airport Information Manual is a AOPA guide to airports in the United States
fields.option_b: The AIM is not published anymore
fields.option_c: The Aeronautical Information Manual is available as part of the FAR/AIM and available online or in print. It provides non-regulatory guidance to pilots.
fields.option_d: The Airman's Information Manual is a reference book published by the FAA.
fields.explanation: Since 1988, the "Airman's information Manual" has been renamed to the Aeronautical Information Manual.

fields.question_text: No pilot may operate a glider unless it has which of the following required documents onboard:
fields.option_a: Airworthiness Certificate, Registration Certificate, Aircraft Logbooks
fields.option_b: Airworthiness Certificate, Registration Certificate, OperatingLimits
fields.option_c: Registration Certificate, Weight & Balance, Bill of Sale
fields.option_d: Airworthiness Certificate, Registration, Operating Limitations, Weight & Balance
fields.explanation:
If you got this wrong, you probably should learn the AR[R]OW mnemonic. 

A.R.O.W.

Aviation mnemonic to remind you of the required documents to make an aircraft airworthy. These documents are checked as part of any pre-flight inspection.

Without these documents the airplane can be brand new and in perfect shape but still be unairworthy. The required documents are:

Airworthiness Certificate.
Registration.
Operating limitations.
Weight and Balance


Airworthiness Certificate:
Each aircraft is issued an airtworthiness certificate as it leaves the factory. This certificate certifies that the airplane meets the design requirements and is safe to operate.

Registration:
Just like the registration in your car. It includes things like Owner's name, registration number, Owner's address, and aircraft serial number.

Operating limitations:
Most people think this means having the aircraft's Pilot Operating Handbook (POH). That is usually true however there is another requirement and that is placards. Some placards are required by Airworthiness Directive (AD) or by the aircraft's type certificate. These placards are considered part of the operating limitations.

Weight and Balance:
A current weight and balance sheet is required to be kept in the aircraft. Also, if the aircraft's weight and balance has a special condition (such as the aircraft is easily loaded out of aft C.G. under normal loading conditions) a loading chart must be kept with the weight and balance.

If you happen to be flying internationally today you will need to add another "R" document and that is a current FCC Radio Station License.

fields.question_text: Federal Aviation Regulations require the glider pilot and the tow pilot to agree upon a general course of action that includes:
fields.option_a: Takeoff and release signals, tow altitude, emergency procedures
fields.option_b: Takeoff and release signals, airspeeds, emergency signals
fields.option_c: Proper radio frequencies, airspeeds, tow direction
fields.option_d: General direction of tow and tow altitude
fields.explanation:
§ 91.309  Towing: Gliders and unpowered ultralight vehicles.

(a) No person may operate a civil aircraft towing a glider or unpowered ultralight vehicle unless

...

(5) The pilots of the towing aircraft and the glider or unpowered ultralight vehicle have agreed upon a general course of action, including takeoff and release signals, airspeeds, and emergency procedures for each pilot.

fields.question_text: Which one of the following thermal indexes predict the best probability of good soaring conditions?
fields.option_a: +2 milibars
fields.option_b: -2 milibars

fields.question_text: Who has the final responsibility for the safe operation of a glider?
fields.option_a: The duty officer
fields.option_b: The aircraft owner
fields.option_c: The pilot in command
fields.option_d: The aircraft operator
fields.explanation:
§91.3 Responsibility and authority of the pilot in command.

(a) The pilot in command of an aircraft is directly responsible for, and is the final authority as to, the operation of that aircraft.

  • The duty officer may have information about the aircraft that you don't know -- check with the duty officer before flying.  However, he does not hold the final responsibility of the glider's operation

fields.question_text: What documents must be in the pilot's physical possession during each solo flight?
fields.option_a: Logbook, CFI endorsements, pilot certificate
fields.option_b: Pilot's certificate, government issued picture ID
fields.option_c: Logbook, pilots certificate, medical certificate
fields.option_d: None, as long as all required documents are accessible at the airport
fields.explanation:
§61.3 Requirement for certificates, ratings, and authorizations.

(a) Pilot certificate. No person may serve as a required pilot flight crewmember of a civil aircraft of the United States, unless that person—

(1) Has a pilot certificate or special purpose pilot authorization issued under this part in that person's physical possession or readily accessible in the aircraft when exercising the privileges of that pilot certificate or authorization. However, when the aircraft is operated within a foreign country, a pilot license issued by that country may be used; and

(2) Has a photo identification that is in that person's physical possession or readily accessible in the aircraft when exercising the privileges of that pilot certificate or authorization. ...

fields.question_text: Minimum Sink Speed is defined as:
fields.option_a: the best speed for thermalling
fields.option_b: the airpseed at which the minimum total drag occurs
fields.option_c: the airspeed at which the glider will lose the least amount of altitude for a given period of time
fields.option_d: the airspeed for penetrtation into a headwind
fields.explanation: Although the minimum sink speed is usually the best speed for thermalling, it is not defined as such.  The min sink speed is the airspeed in which the glider will lose the least amount of altitude over time.

fields.question_text: Define Best L/D Speed
fields.option_a: The airspeed for thermaling
fields.option_b: The airspeed at which the glider will lose the least altitude for a given distance traveled
fields.option_c: The airspeed for penetrtation into a headwind
fields.option_d: the airspeed at which the glider will lose the least amount of altitude for a given period of time
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook, page G-1, 
"Best Glide speed (Best L/D Speed) -- the airspeed that results in the least amount of altitude loss over a given distance."

fields.question_text: Define Maneuvering Speed
fields.option_a: The speed to fly in the pattern while maneuvering to land.
fields.option_b: The speed to fly while between thermals, searching for lift
fields.option_c: The speed above which full or sharp deflections of the controls should not be made.
fields.option_d: The slowest speed at which the glider can safely be maneuvered.
fields.explanation: The regulatory definition is in 14 CFR 25.335  (difficult to parse), but in short, it's the speed where the "full application of pitch, roll, or yaw controls should be confined to speeds below the maneuvering speed;"

fields.question_text: Define maximum structural cruising speed (V<sub>NO</sub>)
fields.option_a: The maximum speed at which the glider should be flown in turbulent conditions
fields.option_b: The maximum speed at which aerobatic flight maneuveurs may be performed
fields.option_c: The speed above which the glider may experience flutter
fields.option_d: The speed above which people may get motion sickness
fields.explanation: Instructors -- I am unable to find any document satisfactorily describing this airspeed in the GFH, AIM or FARs.

fields.question_text: Which of the following best describes the never-exceed speed (V<sub>NE</sub>)?
fields.option_a: The maximum speed that the glider may be flown in smooth air
fields.option_b: The maximum speed that the glider may be flown in rough air
fields.option_c: The maximum speed that the glider may be flown with full control deflection
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
GFH page 4-3
NEVER-EXCEED SPEED (the red line). This is the maximum speed at which the glider can be operated in smooth air. This speed should never be exceeded intentionally

fields.question_text: Under which case would it NOT be mandatory that a glider pilot immediately release from tow:
fields.option_a: The tow plane rocks its wings
fields.option_b: The tow plane and glider experience severe turbulence
fields.option_c: The tow plane cannot be seen from the glider cockpit
fields.option_d: The tow pilot ejects from the tow plane
fields.explanation:
If the towplane rocks its wings, that is the signal to immediately release. 

If the towplane can not be seen from the glider cockpit, formation flight can no longer be maintained, and the glider must release.

If the tow pilot ejects from the tow plane, formation flight can no longer be maintained, and the glider must release.

Severe turbulence is not a mandatory situation where the glider must release, or there would never be any good wave flights out of Petersburg.

fields.question_text: While under tow at a "comfortable" altitude, all of a sudden the tow plane starts a rapid descent to the left.  What should you do?
fields.option_a: Turn the glider in the opposite direction, release the rope and remain clear of the tow plane
fields.option_b: Follow the tow plane, and keep the rope attached so it is not lost
fields.option_c: Return to the airport and land before the tow plane
fields.option_d: Release the rope and remain clear of the tow plane
fields.explanation: If the tow pilot does this, there's not much point in hanging on.  It's most likely the towpilot thought that you released. There probably isn't going to be enough time to turn the glider into the opposite direction before releasing, so just pull the release and continue the flight from there.

fields.question_text: What is the ground signal for stop operations immediately?
fields.option_a: both arms straight up
fields.option_b: both arms straight out and wave both arms over head
fields.option_c: motion with your hand across your neck
fields.option_d: Jump up and down and yell as loud as possible.
fields.explanation: Glider Flying Handbook, page 7-1, Figure 7-1

fields.question_text: Describe how to correct for significant slack in the towline
fields.option_a: make a coordinated turn away from the tow plane
fields.option_b: decend the glider in tow
fields.option_c: extend dive brakes judiciously till the slack is removed
fields.option_d: Allow the slack to be taken up by the tow plane
fields.explanation: This question doesn't offer a right answer (see question #82, which asks for the 'immediate reaction to significant slack line", with an answer choice of "yaw away from the tow plane").  Recommend removing this question from the question bank.

fields.question_text: If, as a student pilot, or as a non-glider rated transition pilot, you have not flown for 90 days since your endorsement was given, you:
fields.option_a: must pass a check ride given by an SSC flight instructor and receive the proper endorsements.
fields.option_b: are legal to fly solo with a verbal OK from your instructor
fields.option_c: must pass both a practical and written exam
fields.option_d: must have your student pilot certificate re-issued
fields.explanation:
§ 61.87   Solo requirements for student pilots.

(n) Limitations on student pilots operating an aircraft in solo flight. A student pilot may not operate an aircraft in solo flight unless that student pilot has received:
(2) An endorsement in the student's logbook for the specific make and model aircraft to be flown by an authorized instructor, who gave the training within the 90 days preceding the date of the flight.

fields.question_text: Spin recovery should follow the manufacturer's recommendations in the POH.  In the absence of that information, what is the "nominal" spin recovery technique for a glider?
fields.option_a: Ailerons neutral, full opposite rudder to break rotation, forward stick to break stall, neutralize rudder, carefully apply back elevetor to return to normal airspeed
fields.option_b: Lower the nose, close speedbrakes/spoilers, full opposite aileron to stop the rotation, when a sufficent flying speed has been reached, gentle backpressure  to a normal glide attitude
fields.option_c: Full opposite rudder to stop the rotation, when a sufficent flying speed has been reached, gentle backpressure  to a normal glide attitude
fields.option_d: Open speedbrakes, full opposite rudder to stop the rotation,when a sufficent flying speed has been reached, gentle backpressure  to a normal glide attitude
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-32

"Step 1 Position the ailerons neutral.  Step 2 Apply full opposite rudder against the rotation. Step 3 Apply a positive and brisk, straight forward movement of the elevator control past neutral to break the stall.  Step 4, after spin rotation stops, neutralize the rudder" The unlisted fifth step is to gently pull the glider out of the dive.

fields.question_text: Shortly after takeoff the tow pilot rapidly fans the rudder of the towplane.  The glider pilot should:
fields.option_a: check spoilers for inadvertent extension
fields.option_b: release immediately
fields.option_c: position the glider to a lower tow position
fields.option_d: Close your canopy - it may be open
fields.explanation:
This signal is not referenced in the Glider Flying Handbook (2003).

The signal is used to indicate that "Something is wrong with the glider"  In this case, either A or D could be considered correct answers.  However, at low altitude "shortly after takeoff" opened spoilers are more critical to the safe continuation of flight than an open canopy.  Check the spoilers first!  Don't release unless you are in a good position to release, and don't confuse the signals of "Release immediately!" (rocking the wings) with "Something is wrong with the glider" (rapid fanning of the rudder).

fields.question_text: Proper traffic clearing procedures should always be used:
fields.option_a: prior to positioning on an active runway
fields.option_b: prior to all turns
fields.option_c: prior to all abnormal maneuvers
fields.option_d: all of the above
fields.explanation: During the practical examination for the private pilot, the examiner will be observing your attention to proper scanning techniques.  But most of all, you should be performing proper scanning and clearing of turns because your life depends on it!  Clear for traffic before taxiing onto a runway (even if you have made the radio call), prior to all turns in flight, prior to any maneuvers such as stalls, slow flight, or steep dives.

fields.question_text: When approaching FRR from the west to land, you hear a power plane announce on the Unicom frequency that he is 'entering downwind' for the active runway, your first action would be:
fields.option_a: Announce by radio that you are in a glider, are landing, and have the right of way
fields.option_b: Proceed with caution, visually locate the power plane, and announce on the radio your entry into the pattern when appropriate
fields.option_c: Immediately select the alternative runway, complete your pre-landing checklist and proceed to execute a pattern for that runway
fields.option_d: Slow to minimum sink speed to extend your time aloft to give the power plane a chance to land
fields.explanation: Many pilots erroneously believe the glider has the right-of-way in this situation; slowing and circling in the pattern increases the liklihood of creating a more dangerous situation; the situation is not sufficiently dire to declare an emergency; glider pilots should always have alternatives when the situation beyond their control changes.

fields.question_text: As a student pilot, while flying in the vicinity of the Front Royal airport, you must have in your possession while flying solo:
fields.option_a: Student pilot certificate and radio license if the glider has a radio
fields.option_b: Student pilot certificate and Class III or higher medical certificate
fields.option_c: Student pilot certificate and a separate photo ID acceptable to the FAA
fields.option_d: Student pilot certificate, logbook, photo ID
fields.explanation:
§61.3 Requirement for certificates, ratings, and authorizations.

(a) Pilot certificate. No person may serve as a required pilot flight crewmember of a civil aircraft of the United States, unless that person—

(1) Has a pilot certificate or special purpose pilot authorization issued under this part in that person's physical possession or readily accessible in the aircraft when exercising the privileges of that pilot certificate or authorization. However, when the aircraft is operated within a foreign country, a pilot license issued by that country may be used; and

(2) Has a photo identification that is in that person's physical possession or readily accessible in the aircraft when exercising the privileges of that pilot certificate or authorization. The photo identification must be a:

A medical certificate is not required per 61.23(b)(1)(ii)

§ 61.23   Medical certificates: Requirement and duration.

(b) Operations not requiring a medical certificate. A person is not required to hold a medical certificate—

(1) When exercising the privileges of a student pilot certificate while seeking—

(ii) A pilot certificate with a glider category rating or balloon class rating; 

61.51  states that a student must have his logbook with him, when performing cross-country flight. Since this is a flight around the airport's area, it is not considered a cross-country flight.   Of course, it's OK to bring along your logbook; it's just not required.

fields.question_text: When flying along the ridge in ridge lift, you overtake a slower glider. Safety required that you:
fields.option_a: Make radio contact before passing
fields.option_b: Pass on the ridge side
fields.option_c: Pass on his left
fields.option_d: Pass on his right

fields.question_text: What cloud is a clue to the presence of wave lift?
fields.option_a: Cumulus
fields.option_b: Lenticular
fields.option_c: Stratus
fields.option_d: Virga

fields.question_text: When during the pre-landing checklist, you discover that the spoilers have frozen in the closed position.
fields.option_a: Use a slip to control glide path
fields.option_b: Extend each leg of the pattern as needed
fields.option_c: Select the longest runway available to you
fields.option_d: Use some combination of all of the above

fields.question_text: What does TLAR stand for and in what context is it usually used?
fields.option_a: Top Level Aircraft Requirements, the legal requirement for defining the minimum equipment list for a glider
fields.option_b: That Looks About Right, an approximation process for aiming something like a weapon
fields.option_c: That Looks About Right, an approximation strategy used to help manage a landing pattern
fields.option_d: Transporter-Launcher and Radar, a type of mobile radar used by ATC in the ADIZ
fields.explanation: The term "TLAR" is not referenced in the Glider Flying Handbook, or FAA AIM.  It is a loose term to indicate that the current position is approximately correct at any point in the pattern.  The term was coined in Tom Knauff's book "Glider Basics", page 97.

fields.question_text: What are the two cases in which it is essential that the glider immediately release from the tow plane?
fields.option_a: Towpilot waggles rudder or towpilot rocks wings
fields.option_b: Glider pilot loses sight of towplane or towpilot rocks wings
fields.option_c: Towpilot waggles rudder or glider pilot loses sight of towplane
fields.option_d: Rate of climb is too slow or radio contact between glider and towplane is lost

fields.question_text: According to the SSC Operations Manual, the normal mid-field downwind position is at _____ ft MSL positioned so that the pilot is looking down at the runway at a ___ degree angle.
fields.option_d: 1500 and the lookdown angle is not specified
fields.explanation:
3.8 Traffic Pattern

The airport management has established, as standard practice that powered aircraft (including the tow plane) will fly left traffic and gliders will fly right traffic.  The normal mid-field downwind position is at 1,500 ft. MSL (800 ft. AGL).  The 1 August 2012 revision does not specify lookdown angle.

Reference: OperationsManual.pdf (skylinesoaring.org) Section 3.8 Page 23.

fields.question_text: A student pilot may log as solo time:
fields.option_a: Only the time when he/she is sole occupant of the aircraft
fields.option_b: Only the time as instructor is not using the controls
fields.option_c: Only the time he/she is flying from the front seat of the aircraft
fields.option_d: A student pilot may not log solo time
fields.explanation:
14 CFR §61.51 

(d) Logging of solo flight time. Except for a student pilot performing the duties of pilot in command of an airship requiring more than one pilot flight crewmember, a pilot may log as solo flight time only that flight time when the pilot is the sole occupant of the aircraft.

fields.question_text: An obstruction (trees) on the approach end of a farmer's field will reduce the usable length of the field by:
fields.option_a: 10 times the height of the tree
fields.option_b: 20 times the height of the tree
fields.option_c: 30 times the height of the tree
fields.option_d: 40 times the height of the tree

fields.question_text: What are two features for proper positioning of the glider flying when the tow plane turns?
fields.option_a: Glider pilot's view of towplane's wheels are on the horizon and glider nose pointed at tail of towplane
fields.option_b: Glider and towplane wings are parallel, nose of glider pointed at towplane's tail
fields.option_c: Glider and towplane wings are parallel, nose of glider pointed at outboard wingtip of towplane
fields.option_d: Glider's yaw string is pointed toward the outside of the turn and glider and towplane's wings are parallel.

fields.question_text: CFR 91.126 provides that '...When approaching to land at an airport without an operating control tower in a Class G airspace area' ... each pilot of an airplane must make all turns to the left unless the airport displays approved light signals or visual markings indicating that turns must be made to the right..' At FRR, the established procedures require power planes to use standard left traffic on both runways but glider traffic use right traffic. Why?
fields.option_a: Because it's easier to "see and be seen" by both gliders and airplanes
fields.option_b: Because the airport directory's remarks section defines glider, helicopter, and gyroplane traffic as right-hand
fields.option_c: Because the airport's management asks us to
fields.option_d: Because it's important to maintain separation from powered aircraft in the pattern
fields.explanation:
• The term "see and be seen" is no longer being used by the FAA for collision avoidance.
  • The Airport Facility directory explicitly states that gliders execute right hand traffic for both runway directions; incoming power pilots referring to the airport directory will have this knowledge before showing up to Front Royal.
  • Airport management indeed asks that gliders fly a right-hand pattern, but this request carries no weight compared to the airport facility directory listing.
  • It is important to maintain separation from power traffic, but there are airports where separation by different pattern shapes is impossible, such as at New Market or Luray, where the patterns are defined as the same for all categories of air traffic.

Reference: Airport Directory entry for KFRR :

fields.question_text: On a solo training flight in the local area, what is the first thing you should do safely free of the tow plane?
fields.option_a: check for traffic
fields.option_b: look for potential sources of lift
fields.option_c: locate the airport
fields.option_d: trim for best L/D speed
fields.explanation:
This question is tricky, and a case could be made for each one of the possible answers. What we really need here is an answer that says "it depends."

"Check for traffic" in our opinion, you should have checked for traffic before releasing -- but certainly checking for traffic is important.  Trimming for best L/D airspeed should only be done if you are heading to a particular location.  If you have released in a thermal, it would not be appropriate to trim for best L/D.  Looking for potential lift is important, but finding the airport is far more critical -- If you are not sure where the airport is, look to the towplane and see where he's going!  Getting lost on a solo flight is no fun.

fields.question_text: For downwind landings, which of the following are NOT true? Downwind landings...
fields.option_a: ...have the illusion of high airspeed
fields.option_b: ...have significantly reduced effectiveness of the rudder
fields.option_c: ...require a steeper approach to landing
fields.option_d: ...have a higher groundspeed at touchdown
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-38

"On downwind approaches, a shallower approach angle should be used, depending on the obstacles in the approach path."

fields.question_text: The main purpose of elevator trim is:
fields.option_a: Prevent high-speed flutter of the elevator
fields.option_b: Make the elevator more effective
fields.option_c: Raise the nose of the glider
fields.option_d: Reduce 'pilot workload' by reducing pressure on the stick
fields.explanation: Glider Flying Handbook (2013) page 2-6 "Trim devices reduce pilot workload by relieving the pressure required on the controls to maintain a desired airspeed."

fields.question_text: The final responsibility for determining whether an aircraft is safe for flight lies with:
fields.option_a: The pilot in command
fields.option_b: The aircraft owner
fields.option_c: A certified inspector
fields.option_d: A certified instructor
fields.explanation:
§91.7 Civil aircraft airworthiness.

(b) The pilot in command of a civil aircraft is responsible for determining whether that aircraft is in condition for safe flight. The pilot in command shall discontinue the flight when unairworthy mechanical, electrical, or structural conditions occur.

fields.question_text: For a 'landing out', is uphill or downhill preferred? Why?
fields.option_a: uphill - provides steeper relative glideslope
fields.option_b: downhill - provides softer touch-down
fields.option_c: uphill- minimizes float and possibility of excessive rollout
fields.option_d: downhill - provides more time for fine tuning technique
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 8-9

"If level landing areas are not available and the landing must be made on a slope, it is better to land uphill than downhill. Even a slight downhill grade during landing flare allows the glider to float prior to touchdown, which may reuslt in collision with objects on the far end of the selected field"

fields.question_text: Proper "scanning" involves:
fields.option_a: Scanning the horizon for anything that moves
fields.option_b: Focusing on a series of distant objects to discern moving objects in peripheral vision
fields.option_c: Focusing on distant ground objects to get your eyes adjusted
fields.option_d: Concentrating on objects in your intended flight path
fields.explanation:
See AIM 8-1-6.  That section in the AIM recommends finding a small portion of the sky, studying it for small objects, and moving on to the next section of sky.  The use of peripheral vision is not useful because aircraft on a collision course will not have any relative motion, and peripheral vision only detects objects with relative motion. 

Focusing on distant objects helps guard against 'empty field myopia', referenced in AIM 8-1-6. 

Concentrating on objects in your intended flight path is no good because a mid-air collision is also possible from the side, as well as from the front. 

Also see AC 90-48C , Collision Avoidance.

fields.question_text: What radio frequency should be used for conversation between two gliders?
fields.explanation:
AIM 4-1-11


Use

Frequency

Air-to-air communication
(private fixed wing aircraft).

122.750

Air-to-air communications
(general aviation helicopters).

123.025

Aviation instruction, Glider, Hot Air Balloon (not to be used for advisory service).

123.300
123.500

fields.question_text: A glider loaded so that the cg is too far aft will:
fields.option_a: Be predisposed to fly too fast, and may exceed Vne
fields.option_b: Be difficult to control
fields.option_c: Have a high sink rate
fields.option_d: Be very unlikely to stall or spin
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 5-11 

"If the glider is loaded so the CG location is behind the aft limit, handling is compromised. The glider is said to be tail-heavy. Tail heaviness can make pitch control of the glider difficult or even impossible.

When the glider is tail-heavy recovering from a stall may be difficult or impossible. When the stall occurs, the tail-heavy loading tends to make the glider nose continue to pitch upward, increasing angle of attack and complicating stall recovery. In extreme cases, recovery from stall or spin may be difficult or even impossible. "

fields.question_text: "Speed to fly" means the correct speed to fly:
fields.option_a: In the pattern, but not before the final approach speed
fields.option_b: To minimize the sink rate of the glider while flying through lift and sink
fields.option_c: In turbulence; the maximum allowable speed while still preventing damage to the aircraft
fields.option_d: in general; covering the most ground with minimum loss of altitude
fields.explanation: Speed to fly is not the pattern speed, even though the normal pattern speed we fly is very close to the maximum L/D speed.  It is also not the speed we use to minimize the sink rate -- that's the 'minimum sink speed'.  Speed to fly is not to be confused with V<sub>NO</sub>, the maximum structural crusing speed -- so it has nothing to do with turbulence.  Speed to fly is the speed that for a given headwind or tailwind, a given lift or sink, maximizes the glide ratio of the glider with reference to the ground.

fields.question_text: Back pressure on the stick upon entering a turn is required to maintain proper airspeed because:
fields.option_a: The lift vector provided by the wing is no longer straight up
fields.option_b: the load factor is reduced in a turn
fields.option_c: The drag is increased by the use of the rudder
fields.option_d: the centrifugal force is acting against the lift vector
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 3-11

"To maintain your attitude with the horizon during a turn, you need to increase backpressure on the control stick.  The horizontal component of lift creates a force directed inward toward the center of rotation, which is known as centripetal force."

fields.question_text: What of the following are NOT one of the signs of an impending stall?
fields.option_a: low indication on the airspeed indicator
fields.option_b: mushy controls, especially rudder
fields.option_c: reducing wind noise
fields.option_d: mushy controls, especially aileron
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-27, 

Signs of an impending stall include the following:

High nose attitude. Low airspeed indication, Low airflow noise, Back pressure on sticks, Mushy controls, especially ailerons, Buffet

fields.question_text: Which of these is not an aspect of crosswind takeoffs?
fields.option_a: Weathervaning
fields.option_b: drifting downwind
fields.option_c: tendency for upwind wing to rise
fields.option_d: none of the above

fields.question_text: Immediately upon release from the towplane, the glider pilot should:
fields.option_a: Make sure the rope is clear, then initiate a level right turn
fields.option_b: Make sure the rope is clear, then initiate a level left turn
fields.option_c: Make sure the rope is clear. Then initiate a slightly descending right turn
fields.option_d: Reduce airspeed to best speed-to-fly
fields.explanation: Barring an emergency, the tow plane will always turn to the left after the glider releases.  By procedure, the glider will always turn right.  Exceptions to this rule are for a low altitude rope break.

fields.question_text: How much should minimum sink speed be increased in a 45 deg banked turn?
fields.option_a: Square root of the 1.4 load factor - 1.2 times
fields.option_b: It remains the same
fields.option_c: Square root of the 2.0 load factor - 1.4 times
fields.option_d: Square root of the 3.0 load factor - 1.6 times
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-33 

"In a 45° banked turn, load factor is 1.4 Gs. The approximate square root of 1.4 is 1.2. "

fields.question_text: What is the immediate reaction to significant slack In the towline?
fields.option_a: extend the spoilers
fields.option_b: yaw toward the tow plane
fields.option_c: gently pull up
fields.option_d: yaw away from the tow plane
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-10,
"Slack line recovery procedures should be initiated as soon as the glider pilot becomes aware of the situation. The pilot's initial action should be to yaw away from the bow in the line"

fields.question_text: What is the first thing to do if the canopy starts opening in flight?
fields.option_a: don't stop "aviating"
fields.option_b: above all else, reach for the flapping canopy
fields.option_c: step on the rudder opposite the canopy hinge
fields.option_d: inform Skyline base of what has happened
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook page 8-11  

If the canopy opens while on aerotow, it is vital to maintain a normal flying attitude to avoid jeopardizing the safety of the glider occupants and the safety of the towplane pilot. Only when the glider pilot is certain that glider control can be maintained should any attention be devoted to trying to close the canopy. If flying a two-seat glider with a companion aboard, concentrate on lying the glider while the other person attempts to close and lock the canopy. If the canopy cannot be closed, the glider may still be controllable. Drag will be higher than normal, so when flying the approach it is best to plan a steeper-than-normal descent path. The best prevention against unexpected opening of the canopy is proper use of the pre-takeoff checklist.

fields.question_text: A glider pilot should do which of the following when flying his final approach into a 20 mph headwind and seems to be undershooting:
fields.option_a: Raise the nose to slow the aircraft to just above stall speed and decrease the sink rate
fields.option_b: Use spoilers
fields.option_c: Lower the nose to increase penetration speed
fields.option_d: Stretch the glide by flying at minimum sink speed

fields.question_text: Which way should you circle when joining others in a thermal?
fields.option_a: It is at the discretion of the PIC
fields.option_b: same direction as others
fields.option_c: counterclockwise
fields.option_d: clockwise

fields.question_text: The Linden VOR is located 4.4 NM SSE of FRR. It is a common approach fix for traffic inbound to lAD, and is also a major Victor Airway intersection.
fields.option_a: Avoid this area completely to comply with the FARs
fields.option_b: Exercise extreme caution in this area since it is used for instrument approaches even in good weather
fields.option_c: This is not a problem because all traffic inbound to IAD is under ATC radar surveillance
fields.option_d: This is not a problem because gliders have the right-of-way over powered aircraft
fields.explanation:
• There are no regulations prohibiting gliders from flying over VORs
  • Just because inbound aircraft are under ATC surveillance does not mean that the ATC is aware of your presence, and it does not mean that the ATC will direct inbound traffic away from your glider in this area.  (Especially if your aircraft is not transponder-equipped)
  • Gliders do not have right-of-way over powered aircraft in all conditions, only when on converging and non-head-on courses (See §91.113)
The best answer for these is 'exercise extreme caution', but it might be even safer to avoid medium to high altitude flights in this area all-together.

fields.question_text: On takeoff on RW 28 on a very cold day with winds of 15kts out of the southwest, the towplane suffers a catastrophic engine failure at an altitude of about 50ft. Without performing the standard signal to you (rocking the wings), the tow pilot releases the towline and dives toward the short grass overrun to RW 28. You are approximately 900ft east of threshold to RW 10. You should:
fields.option_a: Clear the release, pull full spoilers, execute a 180 deg tum to the left and land on the pavement
fields.option_b: Clear the release, pull full spoilers, and land in the area ahead, avoiding the towplane, wherever he is
fields.option_c: Glide to the emergency field on the left, clearing the trees and looking for power lines.
fields.option_d: Execute a 180 deg turn to the left, pull full spoilers, and land downwind on the takeoff runway.
fields.explanation:
Glider Flying Handbook , page 7-5 (in this situation) the glider has insufficient runway directly ahead and has insufficient altitude to make a safe turn, the best course of action is to land the glider straight ahead. After touchdown, use wheel brake as necessary to slow and stop as conditions permit. 

Since the glider still has the rope attached, clearing the release when possible is highly advisable.  You don't want the situation to get worse by getting the nose pulled down by an entangled rope while landing.

fields.question_text: A student pilot must have a logbook endorsement made by an authorized flight instructor for the specific make and model of glider to be flown every
fields.option_a: 30 days
fields.option_b: 60 days
fields.option_c: 90 days
fields.option_d: none of the above
fields.explanation:
§ 61.87   Solo requirements for student pilots.

(n) Limitations on student pilots operating an aircraft in solo flight. A student pilot may not operate an aircraft in solo flight unless that student pilot has received:
(2) An endorsement in the student's logbook for the specific make and model aircraft to be flown by an authorized instructor, who gave the training within the 90 days preceding the date of the flight.

fields.question_text: It is the responsibility of the Pilot In Command to be familiar with which of the following prior to each flight
fields.option_a: Aircraft weight & balance
fields.option_b: Aircraft operating limitations
fields.option_c: Existing weather conditions
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
§ 91.103   Preflight action.

Each pilot in command shall, before beginning a flight, become familiar with all available information concerning that flight. This information must include—

(a) For a flight under IFR or a flight not in the vicinity of an airport, weather reports and forecasts, fuel requirements, alternatives available if the planned flight cannot be completed, and any known traffic delays of which the pilot in command has been advised by ATC;

(b) For any flight, runway lengths at airports of intended use, and the following takeoff and landing distance information:

(1) For civil aircraft for which an approved Airplane or Rotorcraft Flight Manual containing takeoff and landing distance data is required, the takeoff and landing distance data contained therein; and

(2) For civil aircraft other than those specified in paragraph (b)(1) of this section, other reliable information appropriate to the aircraft, relating to aircraft performance under expected values of airport elevation and runway slope, aircraft gross weight, and wind and temperature.

fields.question_text: Signal Knob is approximately 4.3 NM from the center of FRR. Assume your glider has a best L/D of 25:1 and there is no wind. You release from the towplane at 3,500MSL over Signal Knob and turn immediately toward the airport. You arrive over FRR at approximately:
fields.option_a: Field elevation
fields.explanation:
1 nautical mile is 6076 feet ; (you can also use 6000 feet as a close approximation)

Use the following equation to determine altitude used per distance:

=

3500 feet MSL - 1045 feet = 2455. The closest answer is 2450 feet MSL

fields.question_text: On the ASK-21, the yellow triangle on the airspeed indicator marks the:
fields.option_a: Minimum sink speed
fields.option_b: Maximum L/D speed
fields.option_c: Minimum approach speed
fields.option_d: Speed-to-fly
fields.explanation: The ASK-21 flight manual  describes the yellow triangle on page 12 as the "approach speed".  It's simply a coincidence that the Max L/D speed for dual flight is also at that speed.

fields.question_text: There are several good reasons to never leave a glider canopy open when unattended.  A reason unique to the K-21 canopies is:
fields.option_a: The canopies form light concentrating lenses that can start a fire
fields.option_b: A canopy might be damaged by unexpected winds
fields.option_c: To avoid collecting dirt and trash in the cockpits
fields.option_d: Open canopies are more vulnerable to collecting scratches
fields.explanation:
Actually, this valuable piece of information IS in the ASK-21 manual. It is in the Derigging section, pages 43a and 43b:

V.3 PARKING
When parking the glider, the canopies have to be
closed !
When an ASK 21 is parked on an airfield in the sunshine
(this must also be observed during the waiting
time until take-off when the pilots are already
on board ) the canopies must not be left open for
some time.
Depending on the position of the sun and the
intensity of the radiation, the burning-glass
effect of the canopies can cause a slo~ fire in the
area of the instrument panel or the headrest respectively.
Therefore, if you have to store the glider outside,
it is absolutely necessary always to close the canopies
and to cover them with a white cloth.

fields.question_text: A student pilot may log pilot in command time when:
fields.option_a: Only that time when he/she is sole occupant of the aircraft
fields.option_b: Only that time when the instructor is not manipulating the controls
fields.option_c: Only that time he/she is flying from the front seat of the aircraft
fields.option_d: A student pilot may not log pilot in command time
fields.explanation:
14 CFR §61.51 

For category-add on students:

(e) Logging pilot-in-command flight time. 

(1) A sport, recreational, private, commercial, or airline transport pilot may log pilot in command flight time for flights-

(ii) When the pilot is the sole occupant in the aircraft; 

For students with a student pilot license: 

(e)(4) A student pilot may log pilot-in-command time only when the student pilot—

(i) Is the sole occupant of the aircraft or is performing the duties of pilot of command of an airship requiring more than one pilot flight crewmember;

(ii) Has a solo flight endorsement as required under § 61.87 of this part; and

(iii) Is undergoing training for a pilot certificate or rating.

fields.question_text: It is dangerous to stand south of the drainage ditch between the ramp and the taxiway because:
fields.option_a: The taxiway is a landing zone for gliders
fields.option_b: This area is used by taxiing airplanes
fields.option_c: Gliders are very silent on approach
fields.option_d: All of the above

fields.question_text: The main hazard in the ramp area is:
fields.option_a: Taxiing aircraft
fields.option_b: Glider movements
fields.option_c: The possibility of getting hit by a propeller
fields.option_d: The possibility of fire from aircraft fueling
fields.explanation: Once you're hit with a propeller, it's all over.

fields.question_text: When a pilot seated in the cockpit yells “Clear prop!”
fields.option_a: He wants you to clear the ramp for taxi
fields.option_b: He is about to start the engine, watch out for the propeller
fields.option_c: He is asking you to check that the aircraft is untied
fields.option_d: He is announcing that he has completed her pre-start check list

fields.question_text: At KFRR gliders may land:
fields.option_a: Anywhere in an emergency
fields.option_b: On the runway if available
fields.option_c: On the grass if the runway is not available, or on the taxiway if the grass is not available
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation: If there is an issue of pilot and aircraft safety, the glider may be landed anywhere on airport property. However, this does not give license to routinely land on the grass strip adjacent to the published runway, or on the taxiway.  Normal landings should take place on the paved runway.

fields.question_text: Which of the following statements about pushing a glider is NOT correct?
fields.option_a: Gliders at Skyline are typically pushed backwards while on paved surfaces
fields.option_b: Pushing on the trailing edge of a wing or control surface can cause damage
fields.option_c: Pushing backwards in tall grass or across a lip can cause damage to the rudder
fields.option_d: Gliders may only be pushed backwards
fields.explanation: At Skyline we typically push gliders backwards on pavement because it is convenient and safe to push from the nose or leading edge of the wing root and the base of the vertical tail.  Gliders can be damaged by pushing forward on the trailing edge of the wing or any control surface.  Glider rudders can be damaged by pushing backwards in tall grass or across a lip such as the edge of a taxiway.  The best direction to push a glider depends on the type of glider and the situation.

fields.question_text: As you turn onto the downwind leg to land on Runway 28, you see a non-radio equipped airplane already on his base leg and below you.  You should:
fields.option_a: Open spoilers and execute an abbreviated pattern, since gliders have the right-of-way over airplanes.
fields.option_b: Close spoilers, slow to minimum sink speed, and circle until the airplane has cleared the runway..
fields.option_c: Declare an emergency on the CTAF and select the alternate runway on your landing checklist.
fields.option_d: Proceed with caution, announce your position on the CTAF, and be prepared to land on an alternate runway if conflict with the airplane is obvious.
fields.explanation: Many pilots erroneously believe the glider has the right-of-way in this situation; slowing and circling in the pattern increases the liklihood of creating a more dangerous situation; the situation is not sufficiently significant to declare an emergency; glider pilots should always have alternatives when the situation beyond their control changes.

fields.question_text: Never push a glider by:
fields.option_a: The trailing edge of the tail surfaces
fields.option_b: The trailing edge surfaces of the wing
fields.option_c: The canopy
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
• No trailing edges should be pushed, they could damage the surface.
  • Pushing on the canopy can cause thousands of dollars of damage

fields.question_text: Use extreme caution opening and closing canopies because:
fields.option_a: They are fragile
fields.option_b: They are expensive
fields.option_c: They are not easy to replace
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation: the obvious choice is D.

fields.question_text: The ASK-21 rear canopy must remain closed at all times unless someone is entering or exiting the rear cockpit because:
fields.option_a: When the canopy is open, it can set the glider on fire
fields.option_b: Wind can easily damage the rear canopy
fields.option_c: It would damage the glider if someone takes off with it open
fields.option_d: The front canopy cannot be latched shut unless the rear canopy is closed
fields.explanation:
It only takes a few moments for the front or rear canopies to start a fire due to the lens effect. We often stress that the rear canopy is the one that can start fires, but there have been melted front cockpits on ASK-21s, as well. 

B is only true for very strong winds, > 30 mph

C is true, but isn't resolved by a closed rear canopy, it's only resolved by a locked rear canopy

D is incorrect because the front canopy can not be latched unless the rear canopy is latched.

fields.question_text: When ground-towing gliders:
fields.option_a: If towing without a tail dolly, use a second person to push down on the nose or pick up the tail when the glider is turning
fields.option_b: Use a second person on the nose or tail to prevent the glider from rolling into the tow vehicle when towing downhill
fields.option_c: Ensure the tow rope is longer than the half the total wingspan, so if the glider pivots the wing will not strike the tow vehicle
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
A) We can prevent excessive wear and tear on the glider's tailwheel by picking it up while the glider is turning. It's preferable to pick up the tail at the tail of the aircraft, rather than pushing down on the nose.  Pushing down on the nose makes it possible for a sudden acceleration to trap the crew under the glider, or to get hit by the wing of the glider.   If picking the tail up, make sure to do so from the inside of the horizontal stabilizer, instead of the tips.  In all circumstances, a tail dolly is most preferable for ground operations.

B) When towing downhill, such as towards the approach end of runway 10, it is possible for a glider to accelerate towards the tow vehicle, and you may not be able to slow the glider from the wingtip without turning it

C) If the glider starts to pivot and it is close to the tow vehicle, the wingtip may hit the tow vehicle; holding back one wingtip may make the other wingtip swing forward

D) All of the above are true

fields.question_text: When entering or exiting a glider, you should
fields.option_a: wait for the instructor to get in first
fields.option_b: never step directly on the seat cushion
fields.option_c: use the seat straps to pull on for leverage
fields.option_d: never grab the canopy
fields.explanation:
The canopy is the most fragile and delicate part of the cockpit.  Replacing it is terribly expensive!  Also don't touch the canopy because the fingerprints reduce visibility. 

"never step directly on the seat cushion" is also true, but not as important as "never grab the canopy"

fields.question_text: It is important to announce yourself before pushing a glider onto the runway:
fields.option_a: to be sure that no glider is about to land
fields.option_b: to indicate to the tow plane that you are ready to launch
fields.option_c: because doing so assures that you will not interfere with runway traffic
fields.option_d: to add a level of safety -- although you must also clear the area visually
fields.explanation:
D is correct -- making the radio call only adds to the safety, and still you must look for incoming traffic, even if you made the radio call. 

A is incorrect because an incoming glider may not have a working radio.  If you go out to the active runway without clearing first, at least he will be aware of your position and intentions, and be able to act accordingly, but making the radio call alone does not ensure that there are no gliders about to land.

B is incorrect because there are many circumstances when he is aware you are ready to launch without a radio call. 

C is incorrect because making a call does not mean you are not going to interfere with runway traffic.  An incoming plane on final approach will find your call and rolling out onto the runway as an interference with his final approach.

fields.question_text: FAA regulations require that a tow rope must
fields.option_a: be at least 150 feet long
fields.option_b: must be at least as strong as 80 percent of the minimum flying weight of the glider
fields.option_c: must have the appropriate rings on each end
fields.option_d: may not be stronger than 200 percent of the gross weight of the glider
fields.explanation:
A is incorrect because the regulations don't specify any rope length at all. 

B is incorrect because the regulation doesn't specify the percentage against the minimum flying weight of the glider. 

C is incorrect because the regulations don't specify what kind of connections there must be. However, hooking up the tost ring on the Sprite could be disastrous!

Reference: 91.309 Towing: Gliders and unpowered ultralight vehicles.

fields.question_text: The maneuvering speed is shown on the airspeed indicator of the ASK-21 where
fields.option_a: the top of the green arc
fields.option_b: the top of the yellow arc
fields.option_c: the yellow triangle
fields.option_d: it is not shown on the airspeed indicator
fields.explanation:
As a general rule, the top of the yellow arc is not maneuvering speed, but the normal operating range (VNO). Aircraft certified by the FAA follow this convention. The ASK-21 is a different story. The addage of "do whatever the manual says" applies here.  When the ASK-21 was certified in 1980 in West Germany, the convention of VNO at the of the green arc didn't yet apply. 

Gliders certified in Europe since 2001 all share the convention of VNO at the top of the green arc. 

See FAA-H-8083 page 4-3, which lists the colors on airspeed indicator markings. Maneuvering Speed is listed under "Other airspeed limitations".

fields.question_text: SSC uses a designates the official 'IP' (Initial Point) as the beginning of the glider landing pattern:
fields.option_a: There is no official 'point' designated as IP
fields.option_b: You must learn each different instructor's preference of IP, and use that one when flying with that instructor
fields.option_c: For 28 approach, the bend in the road, for 10 approach, use the pond
fields.option_d: about 700 feet perpendicular to the opposite end of the runway you are landing on.
fields.explanation: To avoid teaching landmarks as specific 'crutches' to landing pattern techniques, SSC's policy is simply to enter - and announce - a more general '45 degree intercept' to the downwind leg. Similarly, students are not taught to turn from downwind to base leg based on a ground marker. In general, we support the TLAR pattern technique.

fields.question_text: If the transponder's battery fails in flight, you must:
fields.option_a: keep the transponder on. The regs require it to be on if equipped.
fields.option_b: turn off the transponder by hitting the circuit breaker switch on the instrument panel
fields.option_c: Turn off the transponder at the control head and transponder master power switch, and after the flight recharge the battery
fields.option_d: immediately end the flight.
fields.explanation:
91.215(c) states that if equipped with an operable transponder, it must be on.  Since the transponder is no longer operable, A probably isn't the best answer.  There are provisions in the same regulation allowing for an aircraft under ATC control to continue working with an inoperative transponder along his route.  

91.215 ATC transponder and altitude reporting equipment and use.

 (c) Transponder-on operation. While in the airspace as specified in paragraph (b) of this section or in all controlled airspace, each person operating an aircraft equipped with an operable ATC transponder maintained in accordance with §91.413 of this part shall operate the transponder, including Mode C equipment if installed, and shall reply on the appropriate code or as assigned by ATC.

In order to keep the battery from draining to zero, thus causing the battery to be replaced, it's probably better to just shut off the transponder for the remainder of the flight.   Option B isn't a good choice, given that the transponder should be turned off from the component, instead of the power switch toggle.  Option C is a better choice -- turn off the transponder from the device, and recharge the battery after the flight.

Keep in mind, though that D looks like a pretty good answer, too, because of §91.7 

§ 91.7   Civil aircraft airworthiness.

(a) No person may operate a civil aircraft unless it is in an airworthy condition.

(b) The pilot in command of a civil aircraft is responsible for determining whether that aircraft is in condition for safe flight. The pilot in command shall discontinue the flight when unairworthy mechanical, electrical, or structural conditions occur.

 

Is a malfunctioning transponder reason enough to cause the aircraft to be unairworthy?  Not really.  The wings keep flying, and the controls keep working, so 91.7 doesn't really apply.

fields.question_text: For the TT21 Mode S Transponder installed in the ASK-21, what is the difference between the "Alt" mode and the "On" mode?
fields.option_a: Each setting carries the same function, but the "Alt" mode uses more power.
fields.option_b: The "Alt" mode has functionalities that were originally designed for airplanes under control of ATC. Use of the"On" mode is all that is necessary for our flying club operations.
fields.option_c: The "Alt" mode will respond will respond to ATC with altitude information, the "On" mode will not.
fields.option_d: The "Alt" mode is a special mode where we activate when we expect to be flying at higher altitudes, or in wave.
fields.explanation:
TT21 Mode S Transponder Manual, page 1 -- 

 OFF Power is removed from the transponder. 
 SBY  The transponder is on, but will not reply to any interrogations
 GND  The transponder will respond to Mode S ground interrogations from surface movement radar.
 ON  The transponder will respond to all interrogations, but altitude reporting is suppressed. 
 ALT  The transponder will respond to all interrogations.

fields.question_text: What is a TFR and how does it apply to you?
fields.option_a: Temporary Flight Routing - The ATC can direct air traffic around a certain area
fields.option_b: Transient Foul Route - Aircraft flying through, but not stopping at an area must follow new routing instructions
fields.option_c: Temporary Flight Restriction - A flight restriction that prohibits flight into an area.
fields.option_d: Transport Flight Route - A flight path reserved for air carriers, transport carriers, and jet liners.
fields.explanation:
The AIM and FAR's both discuss this topic: the former in terms of definition and the latter in terms of requirments. It's a very important type of NOTAM.

AIM 3-5-3  and FAR General Air Traffic Operating Rules 91.137  through  91.144 apply. 

You must be aware of and comply with all active NOTAMS. One of several sources of their existance is Flight Service Weather Briefers, 1800wxbrief.com , and http://tfr.faa.gov/

fields.question_text: What is the maximum pressure altitude allowed without supplemental oxygen while operating a club glider?
fields.option_a: 10,000 feet
fields.option_b: 12,000 feet
fields.option_c: 12,500 feet
fields.option_d: 14,000 feet
fields.explanation:
See SSC Ops Manual  para. 3.11   'Members may not fly Club aircraft above 12,500ft MSL without use of an approved oxygen system, and must have a logbook entry documenting training on the oxygen system to be used, signed by a Skyline Soaring instructor.'

FAR 91.211  - which is less stringent than the Club rule - also applies.

fields.question_text: A pilot goes out with his friends on Friday night and consumes a significant number of beers at a bar, finishing the last drink at midnight. He gets a taxi ride home. At 0800 the next morning, he wakes up with a slight hangover, and recovers after consuming a few aspirin. He notices that it's going to be a great soaring day and goes to the airport for a morning glider flight starting at 1000.
fields.option_a: The pilot may fly so long as his blood alcohol content is less than 0.04% BAC by volume.
fields.option_b: The pilot should not fly for 12-24 hours after the overconsumption.
fields.option_c: If the pilot was drinking hard liquor instead of beer, he would be grounded, but beer is OK.
fields.option_d: The pilot had more than 8 hours from drinking until take-off, so it's OK for him to fly.
fields.explanation:
The pilot probably shouldn't fly for 12-24 hours after the binge drinking.
