Just because the regulations state that 8 hours is the minimum time permitted between the consumption of alcoholic beverages to piloting an aircraft doesn't mean it's a good idea.  The spectacular soaring weather MUST NOT be a determination for how fit to fly the pilot is. AIM 8-1-1 states the following: 

 2. A consistently high alcohol related fatal aircraft accident rate serves to emphasize that alcohol and flying are a potentially lethal combination. The CFRs prohibit pilots from performing crewmember duties within 8 hours after drinking any alcoholic beverage or while under the influence of alcohol. However, due to the slow destruction of alcohol, a pilot may still be under influence 8 hours after drinking a moderate amount of alcohol. Therefore, an excellent rule is to allow at least 12 to 24 hours between “bottle and throttle,” depending on the amount of alcoholic beverage consumed.

fields.question_text: Situation: Billy-Bob is getting heavier in his later years. His BMI is 35, but still manages to stay under the maximum weight limit of the gliders at the field.  His wife complains of his constant very loud snoring all through the night.  How could this affect his hobby of aviation?
fields.option_a: His flying career will be over, once he exceeds the maximum allowable weight for our gliders
fields.option_b: He will have a hard time finding an instructor to fly with him, while staying under max-gross weight
fields.option_c: He could have sleep-obstructive apnea, causing him to have chronic fatigue
fields.option_d: His weight alone poses a higher possibility of other chronic ailments such as high blood pressure and heart failure
fields.explanation: While all of these possible answers are true, the most alarming part of this example is the very loud snoring.  Recently, the AIM has been updated to reflect the dangers associated with chronic fatigue caused by a sleeping disorder called sleep apnea. AIM 8-1-e-(4) writes about this, and recommends seeing a sleep study specialist to determine if the pilot has this condition.

fields.question_text: The Club Laptop kiosk user is designed to
fields.option_a: Have full admin privileges
fields.option_b: Prevent logsheet creation
fields.option_c: Allow logsheet management but restrict admin overrides
fields.option_d: Require logout at the end of each day
fields.explanation:
The kiosk user can create/update logsheets but cannot override administrative functions

  • A is wrong: It explicitly does not have admin override powers.
  • B is wrong: Creating logsheets is one of its main functions.
  • D is wrong: It does not require logout.

fields.question_text: If Internet connectivity drops during the day (after initialization), the duty officer should:
fields.option_a: Stop logging flights immediately
fields.option_b: Continue logging flights and allow IndexedDB to queue changes
fields.option_c: Restart the browser
fields.option_d: Switch to paper logs permanently
fields.explanation:
Offline mode queues actions locally using IndexedDB and syncs when connectivity returns 

  • A is incorrect: Logging continues offline.
  • C is incorrect: Closing the browser risks losing queued data.
  • D is incorrect: Paper is not required unless system failure persists.

fields.question_text: If the yellow offline banner appears and you have entered important data, you should:
fields.option_a: Close the browser to reset the connection
fields.option_b: Log out and log back in
fields.option_c: Leave the app open until connectivity is restored
fields.option_d: Re-enter the data on your phone
fields.explanation:
Closing the app while offline can cause IndexedDB data loss

  • A and B risk losing unsynced data
  • D risks duplicate/conflicting entries

fields.question_text: When an instructor is selected for a two-seat glider flight, the passenger fields
fields.option_a: Are required
fields.option_b: Automatically populate
fields.option_c: Ghost out
fields.option_d: Become optional but editable
fields.explanation:
Passenger fields ghost out when an instructor is onboard

  • A is false: They are disabled.
  • B is false: They blank out.
  • D is false: They are not editable.

fields.question_text: A flight with a launch time but no landing time is considered
fields.option_a: Pending
fields.option_b: Flying
fields.option_c: Incomplete
fields.option_d: Finalized
fields.explanation:
Flights with launch time but no landing time are marked "Flying"

  • A is before launch.
  • C is not a system status.
  • D applies to logsheets, not individual flights.

fields.question_text: Cloning a flight copies all of the following EXCEPT
fields.option_a: Pilot
fields.option_b: Instructor
fields.option_c: Passenger information
fields.option_d: Release altitude
fields.explanation:
Passenger information is not copied when cloning.

[Reference:]

fields.question_text: How does one qualify as an Assistant Duty Officer?
fields.option_a: Be appointed by the Chief Duty Officer
fields.option_b: Be a member of the Club for at least 90 days, have a private pilot certificate, and be appointed by the Chief Duty Officer.
fields.option_c: Have a private pilot license, complete the Soaring Safety Foundation's online Wing Runner Course, be a member of the Club for at least six months and be appointed by the Chief Duty Officer
fields.option_d: Complete the Soaring Safety Foundation's online Wing Runner Course, be instructed on-the-job by experienced ADOs and/or DOs, and be appointed by the Chief Duty Officer
fields.explanation:
The requirements for becoming an Assistant Duty Officer are summarized in Operations Manual Section 1.6.10. It's pretty straightforward - show up at the field, be coached by one of the experienced and qualified members on how to help out (e.g., moving aircraft in and out of the hangar and to and from the flightline; launching a glider, generally helping out as needed). You'll also need to be checked out in how to drive the ATV and the Gator. Once you have been approved as an ADO, an icon will be placed on your online "Instruction Record" page that marks you as qualified for this position.

A new member should qualify as an Assistant Duty Officer well within the first 90 days of his membership (although that is flexible, for example, if a member joins late in the season and doesn't have time to get on-the-job training in the first 90 days). And while a Private Pilot certificate is required to be a Duty Officer, it's not for the ADO.

fields.question_text: How does one qualify as a Duty Officer?
fields.option_a: Be a Full (not Probationary) member, hold at least a Private Pilot certificate, meet all the qualifications of the Assistant Duty Officer, and be approved by the Chief Duty Officer.
fields.option_b: Serve as an Assistant Duty Officer for one year and be appointed by the Chief Duty Officer.
fields.option_c: Complete the Soaring Safety Foundation's online Wing Runner course, and pass a test on how to use the Club's computer to manage operations.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation:
The requirements for becoming a Duty Officer are summarized in Operations Manual Section 1.6.9, q.v. Having said THAT, it's also true that it takes considerable on-the-job training to master all the many duties of the Duty Officer (and managing the Club's logsheet program is one of the more challenging ones). Maybe someday we will have a real test for operating the Club's logsheet program (well, there's some questions on THIS program that deal with that!) because it's one of the more challenging things a Duty Officer has to learn.  Once approved by the Chief Duty Officer, an icon will be placed on you online "Instruction Record" page that marks you as qualified for this position.

You DO have to have a private pilot certificate, but also must be a Full member (meaning you've been a Probationary member for at least a year).

fields.question_text: What are the Duty Officer/Assistant Duty Officer hours of duty?
fields.option_b: 9:00 AM - Until operations are over and all equipment secured
fields.option_c: 8:00 AM - Sunset (varies with season)
fields.option_d: One hour after sunrise - one hour before sunset (varies with season)
fields.explanation:
The Operations Manual Section 2.1.1 (q.v.) says arrival at 9:AM until 'everything's done'; however, you should be both aware of variations and flexible because under certain circumstances (e.g., outstanding soaring forecast day, a special operations day) it may be needed to get to the field earlier and/or leave later. Also be aware that the DO (and other crew) should remain 'on duty' until all aircraft are at least accounted for, including members in their private ships who may be on cross-country tasks.

While early starts are nice in the summertime when facing long, hot days, it's a bit much to ask all the time - and it's also not unusual to be putting things away at sunset!

fields.question_text: What GENERAL activities occupy the Duty Officer all day?
fields.option_a: Serves as "Daily Safety Officer", coordinates activities of all other members, manages Club logsheet program for operations, negotiates with FBO, collects money.
fields.option_b: Sets up all equipment for operations, manages ADO activities, establishes launch priorities, notify Potomac Tracon of operational limits for the day.
fields.option_c: Leads daily Safety Briefing before first flights, coordinates use of trainers for instruction with Duty Instructor(s), negotiates runway use with the Fixed Base Operator
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation: That's a lot of things to be in charge of, right? You can find these activities spelled out in a little more detail in the Club's Operations Manual Section 2.2 inter alia, and there's a lot there (go back and re-read it!)

fields.question_text: What should the Duty Officer generally do before arriving and after leaving the field?
fields.option_a:
BEFORE ARRIVING: Contact Duty Instructor, Assistant Duty Officer, and Duty Towpilot to confirm their participation.

AFTER DEPARTING: Finalize the logsheet, including maintenance reports, safety reports, tow plane tach times, and other operational issues.
fields.option_b:
BEFORE ARRIVING: Contact Potomac TRACON and advise them of limits for operations that day. 

AFTER DEPARTING: upload logsheets to the Club's server.
fields.option_c:
BEFORE ARRIVING: Check weather forecast to confirm that you will conduct or cancel operations that day. 

AFTER DEPARTING: Collect payments from the members who flew.
fields.option_d:
BEFORE: Contact Duty Instructor, Assistant Duty Officer, and Duty Towpilot to confirm their participation.

AFTER: Finalize the logsheet, including an operation and safety report.
fields.explanation:
These things can and should be done before leaving home for the field and after returning home that evening. They're intended to start the coordination process with the key people you'll be working with that day (ADO, DI, DT), set in motion any repairs that might need to be made to equipment, and let the members know what they missed!

You cannot determine before you leave home what conditions prevail at the field, so you cannot tell Potomac TRACON what to expect! And you SHOULD upload the logsheet BEFORE you leave the field.

With the possible exception of area-wide hurricanes or snow/ice of such severity that members cannot safely leave home, NEVER cancel operations due to weather without actually BEING on the field! The micrometeorology in the valley is very difficult to forecast, and is rarely comparable to what you see east of the mountains.

The new logsheet software is continually updated while the laptop is online.  There's no obligation to finalize the logsheet while you're at the airfield. You can do that at home, where you're more comfortable writing up the after-operations reports.  You should do the maintenance reports at the field, though.

fields.question_text: Who is responsible for getting equipment set up before operations begin and what equipment is involved?
fields.option_a: The members on the Roster are responsibile for getting the gliders they want to fly out of the hangar and moved to the ramp, the Assistant Duty Officer is responsible for getting tow vehicles out of the hangars and moving the trailer to the correct end of the runway, and the Duty Officer is responsible for setting up the Club computer
fields.option_b: The Duty Officer directs and coordinates with all members present to move all equipment known to be needed for the day from the hangars to the flight line.
fields.option_c: The Duty Officer and Assistant Duty Officer are responsible for moving all equipment from the hangars to the flight line
fields.option_d: The Duty Instructor is responsible for moving the trainers from the hangar to the flight line, the Duty Towpilot is responsible for readying the towplane for flight, and the Assistant Duty Officer is responsible for moving the tow vehicles and trailer to the flight line
fields.explanation:
The Duty Officer can't "do it alone"! 

The precise sequence of equipment movement will vary a bit from day to day, but in general, the DO and ADO should coordinate the actions of all members as they arrive to move gliders, tow vehicles, and the trailer from the hangar to the correct flight line.  On any day of operations, both the Club trainers should be moved from the hangar to the flight line; if it's known at the time that members plan to fly the Sprite, it should also be moved to the flight line; if not, it may remain in the hangar until a member indicates intent to fly it, in which case the DO will assign one more members to retrieve it.

Members who fly the Cirrus are responsible for assembling it.

The Duty Towpilot(s) will take responsibility for moving the towplane(s) from the hangar to the flight line, although in some cases, other members will help move them, under the supervision of the towpilot.

Members who are private owners are responsible for assembling their own gliders (with volunteer help from other members), and the ADO and DO should coordinate the movement of private gliders with Club tow vehicles on the airport. 

Note: Some private owners may push their own gliders from the hangar or use their on vehicles; if the latter, they must coordinate with the DO before driving their vehicles on airport taxiways and ramp and promptly remove the vehicles when the towing is complete.

fields.question_text: What actions should the DO take before commencing flight operations?
fields.option_a: Contact Potomac Tracon, conduct Safety Briefing, set up club computer for operations, get approval for correct operations runway from fixed base operator, identify wingrunners, assure towropes and gliders are inspected, confirm Duty Instructor, Duty Towpilot(s), and Assistant DO are present, confirm VHF radios are functional
fields.option_b: Contact Potomac Tracon, conduct Safety Briefing, set up club computer for operations, identify correct operations runway, identify wingrunners, assure towropes and gliders are inspected, confirm Duty Instructor, Duty Towpilot(s), and Assistant DO are present, hang up flag (if operation from runway 28), confirm all radios are avaialble and functional
fields.option_c: Contact Potomac Tracon, insure that water/beverages are available, conduct Safety Briefing with all members present, set up club computer for operations, identify correct operations runway, identify wingrunners, confirm towropes and gliders are inspected, confirm Duty Instructor, Duty Towpilot(s), and Assistant DO are present, hang up flag (if operation from runway 28), confirm all radios are avaialble and functional
fields.option_d: Contact Potomac Tracon, conduct Safety Briefing, set up club computer for operations, identify correct operations runway, identify wingrunners, assure towropes and gliders are inspected, confirm Duty Instructor, Duty Towpilot(s), and Assistant DO are present, hang up flag (if operation from runway 28), confirm al personal radios are avaialble and functional
fields.explanation:
That's a lot of stuff, right?  You can find more detailed information in the Operations Manual, Section 2.2.1 and the Duty Officer Instructions and Checklist (q.v.).
 
At the beginning of each operating day, the Duty Officer shall notify the Potomac TRACON Supervisor by telephone of the planned start/finish times for glider operations, maximum altitudes expected, and the approximate area of most glider traffic with reference to appropriate local NAVAIDS. (There is some suggested 'patter' you can use on the Club's logsheet program.)

The Duty Officer shall conduct a daily coordination briefing with all members of the duty crew and all available Club members. The Duty Officer will brief the critical information individually to members who arrive after the initial briefing. The Duty Officer will determine the active runway for Skyline operations by coordinating with the airfield manager and the duty tow pilot and duty instructor, will continue to verify that the proper runway is in use, and make appropriate changes in the glider operations as needed. When the glider pattern is changed, he/she should notify all glider pilots of the change, including those who are airborne, via radio. . . . The decision to launch gliders from the currently active end and recover on the other end, versus just ground- towing gliders to the other end for the next launch, is a judgment call left to the Duty Officer after consultation with the airfield manager and the duty tow pilot and duty instructor.

In addition to notifying pilots of changes in the glider pattern, the Duty Officer shall ensure that gliders announce taking the active runway on the radio. Detailed radio operation procedures are included in the Skyline Duty Officer Instructions and Checklist.

Understand that these are the minimum actions to be taken; you will inevitably discover the need for variations due to unique circumstances presented by the day, and your role as coordinator/assignment of the actions of all members present should help you decide exactly how to organize the day for safe and efficient operation.

fields.question_text: What is included in the morning Safety Briefing?
fields.option_a: Introduce fixed base operator (Reggie or Magan), whose responsibility it is to conduct safety briefings at KFRR.  Consult with them to determine what safety-related actions the Club is responsible for.
fields.option_b: Identify duty crew (including augmenting staff like towpilots and/or instructors), weather (including health-related issues such as heat, wind, and cold), operational status of all vehicles, determine who may be flying cross-country, review of signals and emergency procedures, and reminder to pay up before leaving
fields.option_c: Assign the Duty Instructor (alternate: Duty Towpilot) to inform all present of concerns unique to the day that pilots should be aware of
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation:
This is all covered in Operations Manual Section 2.2.1:

2.2.1 Coordination & Communication

Before initiating operations, the Duty Officer shall conduct a daily coordination briefing with all members of the duty crew and all available Club members. The Duty Officer will brief the critical information individually to members who arrive after the initial briefing. Minimum required briefing items include the following:

• Identification of the assigned duty crew, and volunteer augmenting towpilots / instructors • Weather, NOTAMS, and facility status for KFRR and logical alternates
• Physiological considerations
• Status of gliders, towplanes, and tow vehicles

• Contact information exchange for any glider pilots flying outside safe no-lift gliding distance, and retrieve considerations • Initial flying line-up
• Review of launch procedures and signals, and critical tow signals
• Emergency actions and Emergency Response Plan

• Responsibility to confirm with Duty Officer the accuracy of logged flight data, and to settle payments

fields.question_text: Ian Fleming has been a member of the club for several years. One April Saturday he shows up to take an instructional flight. When his turn comes, the DO goes to pull Ian’s name down from the drop-down list, but it is not there. What should the DO do?
fields.option_a: Go to the “Members” tab on the field computer and click on the “Full” tab. Unclick all the boxes for initiation fees, SSC dues, and SSA membership, type in Ian’s name in the box then hit “Add Full Member”. Ian is now ready to take his flight.
fields.option_b: Instruct Ian that there is an issue with his current status with the club and to contact the Membership Officer, Treasurer, Safety Officer, or a member of the Board. Ian is free to fly and the DO can log his flight as a guest to the instructor with Ian as the alternate payer.
fields.option_c: Instruct Ian that there is an issue with his current status with the club and to contact the Membership Officer, Treasurer, Safety Officer, or a member of the Board. Ian is not free to fly unless the conflict is resolved.
fields.option_d: The DO may substitute Ian’s name with his (the DO), allowing Ian to fly, and putting Ian as the alternate payer.
fields.explanation: Ian has been placed on the No-Fly list for non-payment of dues and fees, has not satisfied his requirement to attend the Safety Meeting, or because a actions taken by the Board of Directors.

fields.question_text: A release altitude of “0” (zero) feet:
fields.option_a: Fails validation
fields.option_b: Is valid
fields.option_c: Automatically sets 300 feet
fields.option_d: Is treated as missing
fields.explanation:
Validation requires release altitude to be present, but the number of 0 is still valid.  There's a possibility that the glider and tow plane got launched, and the rope broke, or the launch was otherwise aborted. The glider either lands straight ahead, or continues to roll on the runway.  In either case, a 0 (zero) is a valid entry on the logsheet program. 

Compare that with a launch that has an unlisted release altitude, listed as "-----" on the logsheet program.  A missing release altitude is grounds for the logsheet to fail validation when you're finalizing the logsheet. 

In this case, 0 (zero) and missing "-----" are separate concepts, and treated differently from the logsheet program's perspective. 

In the case of billing for Skyline Soaring Club, there is a hookup fee of 25 dollars.  If the duty officer believes that the conditions of the aborted launch don't merit the hookup fee of 25 dollars, the duty officer can always select "Free Tow" for this flight.

fields.question_text: Visiting pilots are added to the system by:
fields.option_a: Manually typing their name into the flight modal
fields.option_b: Admin-only database entry
fields.option_c: The "Register Visiting Pilot" QR Code feature
fields.option_d: Selecting "UNKNOWN Private"
fields.explanation:
The QR-based Visiting Pilot feature registers them.  It's really important to get your information from the pilot at the day of operations.  The best way to do this is to have the visiting pilot enter their own information.  Duty Officers are notoriously bad at entering the user's name correctly.  An incorrectly-entered name can haunt a user for years-to-come!

If you got this wrong, please re-read the section in the logsheet-instructions document: See the "Visiting Pilot" section under "Finances"

fields.question_text: Which statement(s) are true regarding how the Glider Flying Handbooks required wing runners to signal a glider launch?
fields.option_a: Rotate your right arm in a circular motion
fields.option_b: Rotate your left arm in a circular motion
fields.option_c: Watch for glider pilot to wag rudder before rotating arm
fields.option_d: A or B plus C
fields.explanation: This is kind of a no brainer. It doesn’t matter which arm you rotate or in which direction. All the matters is that the tow pilot can easily see and acknowledge the signal and that there's clear coordination between the glider pilot and the wingrunner.

fields.question_text: Chris, who is not a member of the club, wants to buy a polyester Skyline Soaring Club shirt for his girlfriend. How does the DO complete this transaction?
fields.option_a: Go to the "Finances" tab in the field computer and select "Charges". Select Chris' name from the drop-down box. Type in the $16.00 for the charge. Then select "Clothing Sale" from the Item box. In the comment box
fields.option_b: Go to the "Finances" tab in the field computer and select "Charges". Since Chris is not a member, leave the name drop-down box empty but go to the Alternative Payer box and type in his name. Type in the $16.00 for the charge and select "Clothing Sale" from the Item box. In the comment box, enter a statement indicating Chris purchased a polyester shirt and paid with a check.
fields.option_c: Politely explain to Chris that merchandise can be sold to only club members.
fields.option_d: Go to the "Finances" tab in the field computer and select "Charges". Since Chris is not a member, leave the name drop-down box empty temporarily. Type in the $16.00 for the charge and select "Clothing Sale" from the Item box. In the comment box, enter a statement indicating Chris purchased a polyester shirt and paid with a check. Now go to the “Members” tab and select “Payer”. Type is Chris’ name and hit Add Payer. Now go back to the “Finances” tab, then "Charges" and select the aforementioned input. Find Chris' name that has now been entered in the drop-box.
fields.explanation: As a not-for-profit club, we are not allowed to sell merchandise to those who are not club members.  However, it's not unheard of for a member, perhaps overhearing the conversation or reacting to the needs of one of theri guests, to volunteer to purchase the item and then give it to the non-member . . . leaving no trace of a sale to a non-member on club records.

fields.question_text: In the course of the day Orville Wright is able to satisfy Bob that he can successfully and safely fly the Grob 103 solo. Orville completes a 35-minute glider flight and a safe landing. What can the Duty Office now do?
fields.option_a: Put a nice write-up in the daily operations report calling out Orville as successfully completing his first solo flight.
fields.option_b: In the field computer, go to the "Shutdown" tab and select "Award". Select Orville's name from the drop-down box, select "A Badge" from the award drop-down box, when make a comment regarding what glider was used and any other pertinent information.
fields.option_c: Coordinate with the ground crew to make sure photographs are take of Orville, Bob and the tow pilot.
fields.option_d: Both A. and B, with C optional
fields.explanation: Sometimes the instructor and/or oher crew may want to 'dunk' the new solo with a bucket of water or 'trim their tail' by cutting off the back of their shirt and writing congratulatory comments on it.  While these are traditions, not every new solo may appreciate them! Students who are nearing their first solo should be made aware of the tradition before they show up at the airport with the nicest shirt they own.

fields.question_text: The Visiting Pilot URL:
fields.option_a: Requires login
fields.option_b: Never expires
fields.option_c: Expires after 24 hours
fields.option_d: Works in offline mode
fields.explanation:
The URL disappears 24 hours after creation. 

[ Reference See "Visiting Pilot"]

The visiting pilot mode does not work in offline mode.

fields.question_text: Before creating a new logsheet for the day, what is required?
fields.option_a: At least one flight must already be queued
fields.option_b: The Club Laptop must be connected to the Internet
fields.option_c: The MiFi must be turned on
fields.option_d: All pilots must have signed in
fields.explanation:
The documentation states that starting a new logsheet requires Internet access (Offline mode does not allow starting a logsheet)

https://www.skylinesoaring.org/cms/restricted-documents/duty-officer-instructions/logsheet-instructions/

  • A is incorrect: You cannot queue flights without a logsheet.

  • C is incorrect: Any Internet connection works; MiFi is not mandatory.

  • D is incorrect: Pilot sign-in is unrelated to logsheet creation.

fields.question_text: Scenario: Fidel has arrived at the club with a brand new glider.  It's so new that nobody has had time to enter the details about that glider into the system.  You look for the glider in the pull-down menu, but can't find it. How do you log the flight with Fidel in the logsheet program?
fields.option_a: Refuse to log the flight
fields.option_b: Select "UNKNOWN Private" as the glider.  It should be at the bottom of the list of available gliders.
fields.option_c: Tell Fidel to use another member’s glider
fields.option_d: Create the glider yourself in admin
fields.explanation: D could also be a correct answer only if the duty officer is Ertan Tete (who happens to be a website admin).  Otherwise, just mark it as 'UNKNOWN Private" and let the webmasters update the information later.  Be sure to get the N number of the new private glider, so all of the details can be entered correctly into the system.

fields.question_text: What responsibilities does the Duty Officer not have?
fields.option_a: Maintaining the flight log, make sure the flight line is moving efficiently, and crowd control
fields.option_b: Welcoming and answering soaring related questions from visitors and assist with completing membership forms.
fields.option_c: Provide the primary radio interface between the ground and flight operations.
fields.option_d: Monitor weather conditions and modify ongoing flight activities accordingly.
fields.explanation: The DO is responsible for safe and efficient glider operations. Anything that distracts from that responsibility is not accountable to the DO and should be handled by the ADO or more preferably another club member. The DO is not responsible for visitors, other than making sure they safety is maintained.

fields.question_text: Full-Member Franco has brought a brand-new motorglider to the field.  He has launched his self-launch motorglider, flew around for several hours, and landed back at Front Royal. How do you log this flight?
fields.option_a: Mark the towplane as "SSC Pawnee", List him as the pilot of the glider, and the glider of the towplane. Add a note at the end of the ops report to have the treasurer and webmaster make any necessary corrections.
fields.option_b: Select "Self-Launch" sailplane in the drop down field for gliders, fill in the pilot's name as the tow pilot as well as the glider pilot.
fields.option_c: Select "UNKNOWN Private" for the glider. Select "Self-Launch" as the tow plane.
fields.option_d: Since he is not using any club resources, it is not necessary to log this flight at all.
fields.explanation: Now that we have a few motor gliders in the club that are capable of self-launching, we would like to keep usage stats on them, even if they are not using club resources when flying.  We have created a special tow pilot and glider that are presented in the drop down menus that allow you to select self-launch as the tow plane.  A tow charge won't be applied to self-launch gliders.

fields.question_text: On what promises to be a really good soaring day, several private owners show up, all intending to attempt FAI badge cross-country flights.  George Gliderguy announces that he is attempting a 500K diamond distance. He uses this badge attempt to get early into the queue. After launch, he returns only 20 minutes after takeoff. George asks for a priority relight. Where does George go into the launch queue?
fields.option_a: George gets the next tow, since he is attempting an FAI badge flight (reference Ops Manual 2.4)
fields.option_b: George gets the first tow after all the other pilots who are attempting a badge flight have had one launch, but pre-empts other members waiting for a tow in Club ships..
fields.option_c: George gets the first tow according to his arrival time at the airport, as recorded in the launch queue established at the start of the day.
fields.option_d: George gets a tow whenever there is an occasion when the towplane is not otherwise engaged in towing.
fields.explanation: Well, TECHNICALLY, C is the correct answer. Ops Manual Section 2.4 gives George a SINGLE priority for tow, so if he screws it up then he has to fall back on the 'normal' sequencing rules.  However, if there is a towplane sitting idle and there are no other members who have a priority for a launch (e.g., planning for a badge or record flight), then D could well be chosen.  While the Duty Officer has final authority on the tow priority, he is also free to exercise discretionary good judgement.

fields.question_text: A student and instructor are doing several repeated rope breaks in the same ASK-21. After landing from the first 3-minute flight, what is the most efficient and correct way to log the next one?
fields.option_a: Add a new flight manually and re-enter everything
fields.option_b: Edit the existing flight and overwrite times
fields.option_c: Clone the flight and enter new times
fields.option_d: Leave it unlogged until end of day
fields.explanation:
Cloning copies pilot, instructor, glider, and release altitude while leaving times blank.

  • A wastes time.
  • B destroys the historical record of the first flight.
  • D creates operational risk and increases error probability.

fields.question_text: What actions should the DO take at the hangar to setup the Skyline Base Station radio at the beginning of the operating day?
fields.option_a: Disconnect the base station charger from the 110 volt AC outlet, place the charger in the trailer for transport to the operating position, and assemble antenna poles.
fields.option_b: Disconnect the charger from the radio, turn off the charger, and leave it in the hangar and assemble the antenna poles.
fields.option_c: Disconnect the base station charger from the 110 volt AC outlet, place the charger in the trailer, and transport the base station and antennas to the operating position.
fields.option_d: Disconnect the charger from the radio, turn off the charger, and leave it in the hangar, and transport the base station and antennas to the operating position.
fields.explanation: The charger should be disconnected from the radio, turned off and left in the hangar. The red and black cable coming from the back of the radio is not connected to anything during the operating day. The radios are powered by a battery inside the radio case. The antenna poles should be assembled at the operating position, not at the hanger.

fields.question_text: What actions should the DO take at the operating position (runway 10 or runway 28) to setup the Skyline Base Station radio at the beginning of the operating day?
fields.option_a: Remove the bungee cords holding the antenna and mast sections.  Remove the antenna and fold the black antenna elements away from the white center element so they form a 45 degree angle.  Assemble the two antenna poles and place them in the white PVC pipes attached to the trailer.  Make sure the antenna cables are free and have no sharp bends.  Turn on the chrome power switch located on the jack panel and verify that the green light is on and the fan is running.  Set the frequency of one of the radios to 123.3 and the other to 123.0.  Plug the mike into the mike jack for the radio set to 123.0.  Verify the volume setting of each radio by pressing the “test” button and listening to the noise
fields.option_b: Remove the bungee cords holding the antenna and mast sections.  Remove the antenna and fold the black antenna elements away from the white center element so they form a 45 degree angle.  Assemble the two antenna poles and place them in the white PVC pipes attached to the trailer.  Make sure the antenna cables are free and have no sharp bends.  Turn on the chrome power switch located on the jack panel and verify that the green light is on and the fan is running.  Set the frequency of one of the radios to 123.3 and the other to 123.0.  Plug the mike into the mike jack for the radio set to 123.3.  Verify the volume setting of each radio by pressing the “test” button and listening to the noise.
fields.option_c: Remove the bungee cords holding the antenna and mast sections.  Remove the antenna and fold the black antenna elements away from the white center element so they form a 90 degree angle.  Assemble the two antenna poles and place them in the white PVC pipes attached to the trailer.  Make sure the antenna cables are free and have no sharp bends.  Turn on the chrome power switch located on the jack panel and verify that the green light is on and the fan is running.  Set the frequency of one of the radios to 123.3 and the other to 123.0.  Plug the mike into the mike jack for the radio set to 123.3.  Verify the volume setting of each radio by pressing the “test” button and listening to the noise.
fields.option_d: Remove the bungee cords holding the antenna and mast sections.  Remove the antenna and fold the black antenna elements away from the white center element so they form a 90 degree angle.  Assemble the two antenna poles and place them in the white PVC pipes attached to the trailer.  Make sure the antenna cables are free and have no sharp bends.  Turn on the chrome power switch located on the jack panel and verify that the green light is on and the fan is running.  Set the frequency of one of the radios to 123.3 and the other to 123.0.  Plug the mike into the mike jack for the radio set to 123.0.  Verify the volume setting of each radio by pressing the “test” button and listening to the noise.
fields.explanation: The three black antenna elements should be set at a 90 degree angle to the white center element. Setting them at another angle degrades the performance of the antenna. The mic should be plugged into the jack for the radio set to 123.3 MHz which is the frequency assigned by the FCC to the Skyline base station.

fields.question_text: The DO wishes to set Radio 1 to 123.3 MHz. How is this done?
fields.option_a: Tune the standby frequency (red display) of Radio 1 to 123.0 MHz and the active frequency (yellow display) of Radio 1 to 123.3 MHz
fields.option_b: Tune the standby frequency of Radio 1 to 123.3 MHz and the active frequency of Radio 1 to 123.0 MHz.
fields.option_c: Tune the standby frequency of Radio 1 to 123.3 MHz and press the flip/flop button labelled with a double arrow.
fields.option_d: Tune the active frequency of Radio 1 to 123.3 MHz and press the flip/flop button labelled with a double arrow.
fields.explanation: The radio frequency can only be changed by setting the standby (red display) frequency to the desired value and then loading that frequency as the active frequency (yellow display) by pressing the flip/flop button labelled with a double arrow. The active frequency cannot be set directly.

fields.question_text: The DO wishes to contact a glider which is aloft and ask whether any lift has been found. May the DO use the base station to do this?
fields.option_a: The DO may use the base station radio turned to 123.3 MHz to contact the glider and inquire about lift
fields.option_b: The DO may use the base station radio turned to 123.0 MHz to contact the glider and inquire about lift.
fields.option_c: The DO should ask the FBO to use the UNICOM radio tuned to 123.0 MHz to contact the glider and inquire about lift.
fields.option_d: Neither the base station radio nor the UNICOM radio may be used to make an inquiry about lift.
fields.explanation: The base station radio is licensed by the FCC to use the frequency 123.3 MHz for the coordination of soaring activities between gliders, tow aircraft and ground stations. This would include using the radio to discuss lift conditions. The base station is not licensed to use the UNICOM frequency 123.0 MHz and should not transmit on the UNICOM frequency except in an emergency. The FBO should not be asked to use the UNICOM frequency to make inquiries about lift conditions. The UNICOM frequency is reserved for communication related to airport operations, requests for ground transportation, food and lodging, communications with ground vehicles, relaying certain limited information to ATC and communications related to safety of life and property. The base station radio may be used to receive (monitor) any aviation frequency.

fields.question_text: The Soaring operation has ended for the day and trailer has been placed in the hangar. What action should the DO take with regard to the base station radio?
fields.option_a: The radio power switch should be turned off (green light will go out).  The charger should be connected to the red and black wire coming from the back of the cabinet.  The charger should be turned on and placed on the top of the radio cabinet.
fields.option_b: The radio power switch should be turned off (green light will go out).  The charger should be connected to the red and black wire coming from the back of the cabinet.  The charger should be placed in the trailer or on the floor where it cannot fall.
fields.option_c: The radio power switch should be turned off (green light will go out).  The charger should be connected to the red and black wire coming from the back of the cabinet.  The charger should be placed in the trailer or on the floor where it cannot fall. There should be space around the charger to allow for ventilation because the charger runs warm in the normal course of operation.
fields.option_d: The charger should not be connected when the radios are not in use.  The charger is used to charge the radios when they are in operation.
fields.explanation: The charger should not be placed on top of the radio cabinet because of the danger that it could fall and be damaged. The charger runs warm during operation and for this reason there should be space for ventilation around it. The radios are powered by batteries and so the charger is disconnected and left in the hanger during the operating day. The charger is connected to the radios when the trailer is returned to the hanger at the end of the day.

fields.question_text: If you lift the truck bed of the Gator (by pressing the release on the front driver's side corner of the truck bed), you will get this view.  The yellow cap in this picure is for . . . .
fields.option_a: Filling the gas tank
fields.option_b: Filling the transmission fluid
fields.option_c: Filling the engine oil reservoir
fields.option_d: None of the above
fields.explanation: The engine oil level should be checked every morning when getting the equpment out of the hangar.  This cap also contains the oil dipstick.

fields.question_text: The larger orange knob in this picture of the Gator is . . .
fields.option_a: Gear selection lever
fields.option_b: Parking brake lever
fields.option_c: Four-wheel lockout lever
fields.option_d: Truck bed release lever
fields.explanation: It has three positions - that way, the other way, and neutral.  The smaller knob locks the differential if the Gator gets stuck and a wheel is spinning (do not operate in the locked position normally!). You can also see the fuel tank guage, the round white dial just below the parking brake hanlde.

fields.question_text: Wieght and balance calculations should be performed prior to every flight. What is the maximum weight specified in the ASK-21 Flight Manual?
fields.option_a: 792 lbs
fields.option_b: 902 lbs
fields.option_c: 1140 lbs
fields.option_d: 1320 lbs
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), Section II.6

Empty Weight approx 792 lbs

Max weight of all non-lift producing memebers 902 lbs

Max all weight 1320 lbs

fields.question_text: Weight and balance calculations should be perfomed prior to every flight. What is the maximum payload (yes...maximum means the parachute weight counts) for the front seat in the ASK-21?
fields.option_a: 154 lbs
fields.option_b: 200 lbs
fields.option_c: 242 lbs
fields.option_d: 286 lbs
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 13, section II.8.

Minimum payload is 154 lbs...max is 242 lbs...

fields.question_text: Flight performace is adversealy affected by precepitation. What does the ASK-21 Flight Manual recommend for final approach if it is raining?
fields.option_a: The Flight Manual is silent on this matter
fields.option_b: Slip the glider for better inflight visibility
fields.option_c: Add 5 knots to your approach speed as a safety margin
fields.option_d: Reduce your approach speed by 5 knots as a safety margin
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 23, Section III.3.

As a safety margin add 5 knots to the approach speed

fields.question_text: It is permissible to launch without a wing runner. If you are departing from an area of high grass or rough ground, what is your immediate action procedure in the event of ground looping?
fields.option_a: Release the tow rope
fields.option_b: Raise the wing
fields.option_c: Lift off sooner to get airborne
fields.option_d: Always use a wing runner in these conditions
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 25, Section III.5

Release the tow rope immediately

fields.question_text: You have landed out and are preparing to fly home the following morning. The gliderport you are departing from is unfamiliar with the ASK-21. In your preflight briefing with the tow pilot, after you have visually inspected the tow rope, s/he asks you if there is a maximum speed for your ship on tow. What say you?
fields.option_a: Yes...but it exceeds your tow plane's capabilities
fields.option_b: No..I'm a great pilot on tow so just fly and I can handle whatever you can give me
fields.option_c: Yes...thank you for asking! 97 kts for the launch this morning, please
fields.option_d: Yes...thank you for asking! 75 kts for the launch this morning, please
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 32, IV.4

Max tow speed is 97 kts...112 mph

Most favorable tow speed during climb is 50-75 kts...56-87 mph

Lift off occurs at approx 40 kts or 47 mph

fields.question_text: Wilbur Wright used the term "stall" for the first time in 1904 when describing a climbing turn he witnessed Orville perform (the two agreed early on not to fly together so if something happened the other would carry on the work. But I digress). Does the use of airbrakes in the pattern increase or decrease the stall speed of the ASK-21?
fields.option_a: Decreases your stall speed by about 1.6 kts because you are slowing down
fields.option_b: Increases your stall speed by about 1.6 kts
fields.option_c: Neither...stalls don't occur at the lower speeds we fly in the pattern
fields.option_d: The ASK-21 has spoilers so it doesn't matter
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 34, IV.8

The airbrakes increase the stalling speed by about 1.5 kts or 3 km/h.

fields.question_text: What are the prohibited aerobatic manuevers in the ASK-21?
fields.option_a: All abrupt aerobatic maneuvers
fields.option_b: Loop forward (outside loop)
fields.option_c: Tail sliding
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 36

All of the above.

fields.question_text: Is it permissible to fly the ASK-21 solo from the back seat?
fields.option_a: Sure...just make sure you have ballast in the front seat
fields.option_b: Never
fields.option_c: Only if the SSC DO authorizes the flight
fields.option_d: Always
fields.explanation:
ASK-21 Flight Manual (9 Mar 83), page 49

Never fly the glider from the rear seat only.

fields.question_text: The ASK-21 Flight Manual authorizes aerobatic manuevers. Another authoritative source document are the Federal Aviation Regulations (FARs). FAR 91.307 provides the guidance pilots need on performing aerobatics. Is that correct?
fields.option_a: True
fields.option_b: False
fields.option_c: N/A
fields.option_d: N/A
fields.explanation:
False.

FAR Sec 91.307, Parachutes and Parachuting. This FAR describes intentional manuevers that exceed certain parameters, but the intent of this FAR is just about parachutes NOT about aerobatics.

Go to FAR Sec 91.303, Aerobatic Flight, to find definitive guidnace for pilots flying acro.

fields.question_text: The ASK-21 is known world-wide as the best sailplane available for initial flight training through solo to certification and is for fun soaring. It has a fair glide ratio (34-to-1) and a low minimum sink rate that allows the "K-21" to climb in weak thermals. But you can't fly it if you break the canopy. Which are important "Canopy Rules" for handling the K-21?
fields.option_a: Closed and locked at all times
fields.option_b: Close rear canopy first and always close them ground towing
fields.option_c: Never lift a canopy by the plexiglass or the open vent window
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
All of the above...plus:

Do not scratch the canopies with rings, watches, belt buckles, cellphones...

When sitting in the glider keep one hand up holding the canopy.

Rear canopy closes first...both latches completly forwards and "clicked"

Canopy hinges are easily damaged in the wind.

fields.question_text: Ground handling is an important aspect of our sport. The wingspan of the ASK-21 is 17 meters/56 feet. This leaves little room with the hangar openings. What are important ground handling techniques to ensure our operation runs smoothly and safely?
fields.option_a: Helpers give a "thumbs up" at each wingtip (better than a verbal)
fields.option_b: Push gently down on the nose to lift the tailwheel--DO NOT SKID the tailwheel sideways
fields.option_c: Never leave the glider with the removable tail wheel dolly attached
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
Moving the ASK-21 means we usually push it backwatds by the nose or wing root

Both canopies fully closed

Don't try to turn the glider by the wingtip without having a helper push the nose down

To push the glider forward, leave the canopies closed and push from behind the wing, palms flat on top of the wing, near the spar

fields.question_text: Ground handling is an important aspect of our sport. After you have completed your Before Takeoff Checklist and are marshalled into position on the active runway, it is easy to become distracted. Tow plane, wing runners, radios, tow rope last minute inspection, and making sure the glider is pointed in the right direction. As the PIC, you have the task to retain Situational Awareness on all of this activity and to be directive, as needed, to ensure safe operations. Why is ensuring pre-takeoff runway alignment so important in the ASK-21?
fields.option_a: Because the prevailing winds will shift
fields.option_b: Because with low speeds without rudder and elevator authority, the K-21 will roll wherever it is pointed
fields.option_c: Because the prop wash from the tow plane will affect lift off speed
fields.option_d: Because you are solo the rear canopy may come unlatched
fields.explanation:
At the start of the takeoff roll the glider will go in the direction it is pointed.

The same affect occurs during rollout so landing alignment is equally important. Aim the glider at the center of the landing zone. Try to hold the nose up at touchdown, but it will quickly slow and dip over onto the nosewheel. With only a demonstrated crosswind of 8 kts, the ASK-21 will "weathervane" into the wind (even when on the nosewheel) as the rudder is small. 

Don't relax on rollout. Use the wheelbrake (full aft airbrake) as much as required. Continuious hard braking may overheat the brake pads, reducing effectiveness.

fields.question_text: The ASK-21 is advertised as being reluctant to spin with two people, but you are light and flying solo.  In your thermal today, you try to turn the K-21 with excess rudder to "tighten things up a bit" in gusty conditions, and suddenly the skidding turn becomes an unexpected drop of the inside wing, with extreme yaw into the turn.  You neutalize the stick and apply full rudder opposite the direction of rotation, and before you can really understand what just happened you find the nose pointed steeply down and airspeed increasing rapidly, still with a lot of bank.  What do you do now?
fields.option_a: Continue to hold anti-spin controls - Opposite rudder, neutral aileron, pause, stick slowly forward to neutral
fields.option_b: Recover from the spiral dive - Relax back pressure, roll wings level, then stick smoothly aft until nose is above the horizon, rudders as necessary to keep yaw string close to center
fields.option_c: Increase back pressure on the stick until nose is above the horizon
fields.option_d: Deploy the parachute
fields.explanation:
See ASK-21 POH page 22 section III.2, "Recovery from Spin"

See ASK-21 POH page 33 section IV.6, "Slow Speed Flight, Wing Dropping and Spins"

See GFH chapter 8 page 8-15, "Spiral Dives"

In this scenario, the aircraft has already stopped spinning and airspeed is increasing, so:

a) not appropriate to continue to hold anti-spin controls

b) a spiral dive, or nose-low unusual attitude, needs to be addressed quickly to ge the nose above the horizon without exceeding max allowable speed or g-loading

c) increasing back pressure before levelling the wings may only tighten the spiral, allow airspeed to increase, and eventually exceed allowable g-loading

d) there is no parachute

fields.question_text:
Getting gliders in and out of the hangars is an important responsibility for the Duty Officer to supervise.  The wingspan of the trainers is about 17 meters (56 feet), which is wider than the hangar doors.  To get the K-21 properly into the hangar, you should:

  1. Start by placing the fuselage dolly over the small red painted rectangular symbol on the ramp just outside the hangar - (Figure 1)
  2. Put the tail dolly on AND the fuselage dolly under the glider while outside the hangar (Figure 2)
  3. Have at least two helpers (three is better)
  4. Maneuver into hangar with gliders's right wing first, under the towplane's wing (Figure 3)
  5. Make sure fuselage dolly is on marked square like this: (Figure 4) 
Figure 1 Figure 2
Figure 3 Figure 4
fields.option_a: All of these
fields.option_b: II, III, IV
fields.option_c: II, III, IV, V
fields.option_d: I, II, III, IV
fields.explanation:
Number one is a trick question!  That's actually the mark from which you start putting the GROB into the hangar, by putting the main gear on that rectangle!

Also number five!  That's actually the SPRITE on ITS dolly!  Here's what the K-21 dolly position should look like when properly in the hangar:

fields.question_text:
Getting gliders in and out of the hangars is an important responsibility for the Duty Officer to supervise.  The wingspan of the trainers is about 17 meters (56 feet), which is wider than the hangar doors.  To get the Grob 103 in the hangar properly, you should:

  1. Start by placing the fuselage dolly over the small red painted rectangular symbol on the ramp just outside the hangar - (Figure 1)
  2. The tail dolly can be on or off; if off, maneuvering it in the hangar requires the nose to be pushed down. (Figure 2)
  3. Have at least two helpers (three is better), one on glider's right wing (Figure 3), one on the nose, and one on glider's left wingtip.  Ideally, another one guards the horizontal tail.
  4. Maneuver into hangar by pushing nose so that main gear tracks the dotted red line, aim to put main gear on this mark in hangar: (Figure 4)
  5. Be careful to not hit horizontal tail on sidewall of hangar

 

Figure 1 Figure 2
Figure 3 Figure 4
fields.option_a: All of these
fields.option_b: I, III, IV, V
fields.option_c: II, III, IV, V
fields.option_d: I, III, V
fields.explanation:
No tricks here . . . this is pretty straightforward.  Notice that the guy holding the glider's right wing is actually using his body to insure that the leading edge of the wing cannot be damaged by striking the hangar door frame!

And it's important to make sure the main wheel follows the dotted line precisely - - otherwise, the right wing tip might not clear the back of the hangar, and/or the horizontal tail may strike the side wall.

Oh, and always keep the glider's right wing down, because the left wing has to clear the Sprite!

fields.question_text: The best order for putting aircraft back in the hangar is:
fields.option_a: Grob, K-21, Sprite
fields.option_b: Grob, Sprite, K-21
fields.option_c: Sprite, K-21, Grob
fields.option_d: K-21, Grob, Sprite
fields.explanation:
Actually, the only rather critical sequencing is that the Sprite needs to go in before the Grob - the K-21 can pretty well fit into any sequencing!

Now, getting them out is pretty obvious - - gotta get the Grob out before the Sprite!

fields.question_text: Canopies on gliders should always be closed when the machine is unattended - gusts of wind are obvious hazards for any glider.  One of our gliders, the K-21, is particularly vulnerable to a unique hazard, which is:
fields.option_a: The locking pins can be inadvertently left extended, causing damage when the canopy is closed
fields.option_b: Open rear canopy exposes the instrument panel to damaging sunlight
fields.option_c: Closed canopies allow the buckles on the harness to get too hot
fields.option_d: Open canopies have caused fires in the cockpit
fields.explanation: The rear-seat canopy forms a convex lens whose focal point is right on the rear seat's pilot headrest!  (It can work in reverse, with the front canopy burning the instrument panel in the front seat.) Recently, the headrest was replaced with a non-flammable cloth, but this shouldn't keep you from getting in the habit of closing the canopy any time you walk away from the glider. The burning headrest event does not have to be strong sunlight, as this can happen on an overcast day. Answer B is incorrect because the front seat canopy is possible to get too hot when it's left open. Answer C is annoying, but not something that can cause permanent damage. Very hot belt buckles are one reason why we have silver aluminized canopy covers for both the Grob and the K-21. These can be used to block the sunlight in the cockpit when the glider is going to be sitting on the ramp for any significant waiting period on a sunny day.

fields.question_text:
Billy-Bob just did his first solo in a high performance glider, the Discus CS. While attempting to stop, the glider pitched over and scraped the nose on the ground, causing a big ugly scrape, removing the outer layer of gelcoat.  What are some of the contributing factors that could have caused this? 

  1. Billy-Bob's pilot weight was too high, and he didn't have the appropriate tail ballast
  2. Billy-Bob didn't apply aft-stick during roll-out
  3. Billy-Bob landed too fast
  4. Billy-Bob landed too slow
  5. Billy-Bob forced the landing onto the pavement
  6. Billy-Bob applied the brakes too abruptly
fields.option_a: I, II and III
fields.option_b: I, II and IV
fields.option_c: II, IV and V
fields.option_d: I, II, III, VI
fields.explanation:
Billy-Bob probably has gotten in the habit of landing main-wheel only, and not holding back-stick on the roll-out. This is a terrible habit, and should be roundly discouraged when flying the Discus or other tailwheel gliders.  In order to minimize the chances of nosing-over, hold back-stick during roll-out, land at as low a speed as possible, and gently apply the wheel brakes.  Contributing factors are very heavy pilot weight and forward CG. 

Remember, on roll-out that stick should be full-aft! This is not the SGS 1-36, and there is no nose skid!

fields.question_text:
During take-off, Billy-Bob suddenly kited the Discus CS several feet above the tow plane, once the glider attained flying speed. What are some of the contributing factors that might have caused this? 

  1. Very low pilot weight
  2. Very high pilot weight
  3. Billy-Bob did not get the tail wheel off of the ground during the acceleration phase of the take-off
  4. Billy-Bob was distracted by the Oudie complaining about a PowerFLARM proximity warning and looked away
  5. Low tire pressure
fields.option_a: I, III, and IV
fields.option_b: III, and IV
fields.option_c: II, III, and V
fields.option_d: IV and V
fields.explanation:
1. Very low pilot weight
    1. Low pilot weight could cause the glider to tend to be less nose heavy, and for a given elevator configuration, could cause the glider to get higher faster. Probably not enough to be a significant factor, though. 
  2. Very high pilot weight
    1. For a given elevator configuration, the pilot would need more back stick to get the nose off of the ground. Probably not a contributing factor.
  3. Billy-Bob did not get the tail wheel off of the ground during the acceleration phase of the take-off
    1. This is the biggest gotcha when transitioning to the Discus CS, or other tailwheel-equipped gliders, after training in the ASK-21 and Grob 103. If you do your take-off roll with the tail on the ground for the whole time, you will be unpleasantly surprised. Once the tow plane and glider have accelerated to the appropriate speed, the glider will very suddenly develop lift, and will balloon into the air.  The novice pilot might not notice this until he's several feet off of the ground, and then has to take immediate action, having to dive down into position. Best solution is to plan to get that tail wheel off the ground as soon as practical during your take-off roll. If you have a cross-wind, try to keep the tail on the ground for better directional stability. 
  4. Billy-Bob was distracted by the Oudie complaining about a PowerFLARM proximity warning and looked away
    1. This is fairly common. Sometimes a plane with a transponder can be located nearby, to cause the PowerFLARM to beep when it's not welcome. Be sure to ignore any extraneous inputs while on that take off roll. It doesn't matter if your seat is on fire, you have a tick on your leg, or your PowerFLARM is beeping, keep that eye on the towplane! 
  5. Low tire pressure
    1. I can't believe you selected this.

fields.question_text: During Assembly, Billy-Bob has inserted the left wing and left it on the wing stand. Jimmy-George is holding the right wingtip. Jimmy-George inserts the right wing. The left wing pops out when Jimmy-George pushes in on the wingtip. What is the most obvious rookie mistake to cause this problem?
fields.option_a: Billy-Bob forgot to put the spar-pin half-way in, during assembly
fields.option_b: Billy-Bob put the spar-pin in all the way, during assembly
fields.option_c: Billy-Bob just needs to go to the left wing and push, that's just the way this glider is. The spar pin goes in only after the wings are fully positioned.
fields.option_d: Billy-Bob has the spoilers unlocked, and needs to lock them
fields.explanation:
The Schempp-Hirth gliders Ventus, Discus, Duo Discus, Nimbus and Arcus all have only spar pin. You always assemble the left wing first.  After inserting the left wing, make sure to put the spar pin in only half-way. This keeps the left wing from popping out when Jimmy-George pushes in on the wingtip. The left wing won't pop out because the back panel is shaped in away to prevent the left wing plus spar pin from moving outboard when the wing spar interlocks engage. 

B is wrong because if you put the spar pin in all the way, you won't get the wing more than halfway in. The insertion of the right wing will bang on the fully-inserted spar pin, and you won't make any progress. 

C is the answer that many people think is right. If you have this problem at a contest, the experienced pilots will laugh at you for being such a rookie. Don't be a rookie. 

D could be right if the spoilers were locked. When the spoiler handle is in the lock position, it makes it very difficult to insert the wings. Make sure the spoilers are unlocked and that the water dump valve is closed when attempting to assemble the wings.

fields.question_text: Risks associated with flying the Discus CS in freezing temperatures include:
fields.option_a: Possibility that controls may freeze and bind, especially the airbrakes.
fields.option_b: Possibility that water in ballast tanks could freeze, causing damage and making the glider no longer airworthy.
fields.option_c: Possibility that dump valves could freeze, making it impossible to get rid of ballast.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation:
A. POH section 4.9 discusses flight at freezing temps and suggests use of Vaseline on the mating surface of airbrakes.

B. POH sections 2.7 and 4.7 discuss flight with water ballast, and states ballast must be dumped before going below 36 deg F. Table on page 22c lists standard lapse rate altitudes and surface temps where freezing is likely.

C. Dump valves could freeze first, though not specifically addressed in the POH.

fields.question_text: To lower the landing gear in the Discus CS:
fields.option_a: Unlock the black handle at the right edge of the seat pan, push forward and engage in the front recess.
fields.option_b: Unlock the black handle at the right edge of the seat pan, pull back and engage in the rear recess.
fields.option_c: Push the blue handle on the left side of the cockpit forward and past the over-center lock.
fields.option_d: Push the sliding red knob on the right canopy rail full forward and lock into place.
fields.explanation:
A. POH section 1.2 (6) page 7 describes procedures for lowering gear, but contains a typo corrected by hand. This position is the reverse of the procedure for the Cirrus, by the way.

B. This raises the gear.

C. This locks the airbrakes closed.

D. This unlocks the canopy at the hinge side so it can be jettisoned.

fields.question_text: To adjust the elevator trim on the Discus CS:
fields.option_a: Loosen the green knob on the left at the seat mold to reposition the elevator trim tab, then tighten in place.
fields.option_b: Move the green knob on the left at the seat mold line inwards to release, move to adjust spring tension, release to lock.
fields.option_c: Move the black ball-shaped knob on the right cockpit wall forward or aft to the desired position.
fields.option_d: Move the blue lever on the left hand side of cockpit forward or aft to the desired position.
fields.explanation:
A. There is no trim tab on the elevator.

B. POH section 1.2 (12) page 8 describes the procedure adjusting spring-loaded elevator trim.

C. The black ball on the right cockpit wall is for opening and closing water ballast dump valves.

D. The blue lever is for airbrakes.

fields.question_text: The maximum permitted speed in the Discus CS is:
fields.option_a: 151 knots at sea level, less above 10,000 ft MSL.
fields.option_b: 135 knots at all altitudes.
fields.option_c: 135 knots at sea level, less above 14,000 ft MSL.
fields.option_d: 119 knots at all altitudes.
fields.explanation:
A. 151 knots is Vne for the ASK-21. You must have gotten confused with that. 

B. POH section 2.4 page 19 lists speed limits by altitude.  Plotting these shows the break from 135 knots is at 14,000 ft MSL.

C. POH section 2.4 page 19 lists speed limits by altitude.  Plotting these shows the break from 135 knots is at 14,000 ft MSL.

D. 119 knots is the Vne for the Cirrus. You must have gotten confused with that.

fields.question_text: The yellow triangle on the airspeed indicator of the Discus CS shows:
fields.option_a: 54 knots, best L/D speed without ballast.
fields.option_b: 51 knots, beginning of the green arc (normal operating range).
fields.option_c: 55 knots, recommended approach speed without ballast.
fields.option_d: 62 knots, recommended approach speed at maximum allowable gross weight.
fields.explanation:
A. 54 knots is close to best L/D without ballast, but that is not where the yellow triangle is and is not what the yellow triangle means.

B. 51 knots is 1.1 multiplied by the clean stall speed. It is also the beginning of the green arc, but that is not where they yellow triangle is.

C. 55 knots is a recommended pattern speed in the Skyline Soaring ops manual, but is not related to the yellow triangle.

D. POH page 18 has a table that shows the meaning of airspeed indicator markings, 62 knots is for approach speed at max weight.

fields.question_text: Maximum permitted speed in rough air, and maneuvering speed in the Discus CS are:
fields.option_a: 81 knots for rough air, 97 knots for maneuvering.
fields.option_b: 97 knots for rough air, 108 knots for maneuvering.
fields.option_c: 108 knots indicated airspeed for both.
fields.option_d: 108 knots true airspeed for both.
fields.explanation:
A. 81 knots is max winch speed, 97 knots max aero tow.

B. 97 knots is max aero tow speed.

C. POH section 2.4 page 19 has a table for airspeed limits, 108 knots indicated for both. 

D. Not true airspeed.

fields.question_text: In the Discus CS, manueuvering load limits must not exceed:
fields.option_a: Positive 3.5 G's with the airbrakes open.
fields.option_b: Positive 5.3 G's or negative 2.65 G's at maneuvering speed.
fields.option_c: Positive 4.0 G's or negative 1.5 G's at Vne.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation: POH seciton 2.5 page 20 lists the maneuvering load factor limits, all these apply.

fields.question_text: With a 16-lb paracute, the maximum allowable pilot weight in the Discus CS is:
fields.option_a: 242 pounds (110 kg).
fields.option_b: 226 pounds.
fields.option_c: 226 pounds, unless tail ballast is carried.
fields.option_d: 154 pounds (70 kg).
fields.explanation:
The maximum cockpit weight, including parachute, is 242 lbs (110 kg), per POH section 2.7 page 21.

A. Does not include the weight of the parachute.

B. Is the max allowable pilot weight after subtracting the mandatory parachute.

C. Tail ballast changes CG, but does not allow higher cockpit weight.

D. 154 (70 kg) pounds is the minimum cockpit weight, including parachute (138 pounds plus parachute).

fields.question_text: The CG location in Discus CS 9Y without water ballast, for a 170-pound pilot plus 16-pound parachute (186 pounds combined), is:
fields.option_a: Within limits, at 20% of allowable aft CG range.
fields.option_b: Within limits, at 50% of allowable aft CG range.
fields.option_c: Within limits, at 80% of allowable aft CG range.
fields.option_d: Outside allowable limits.
fields.explanation:
See cockpit card CG loading chart:

A. 20% of allowable aft CG range is for maximum pilot weight of 226 pounds plus parachute.

B. 50% of allowable aft CG range is for pilot weight of 186 pounds plus parachute.

C. 90% of allowable aft CG range is for minimum pilot weight of 138 pounds plus parachute.

D. Without water ballast, design cockpit load of 154-242 pounds (70-110 kg) including parachute remains within allowable CG range.

fields.question_text: The Discus CS procedure for spin recovery is:
fields.option_a: Apply full opposite rudder, stick full forward, when rotation stops recover from dive.
fields.option_b: Apply full opposite rudder against direction of spin, ease control stick forward until rotation stops, centralize rudder and pull out smoothly from dive.
fields.option_c: Apply full rudder against direction of spin, stick full aft momentarily then stick forward until rotation stops.
fields.option_d: Neutralize all controls until spin stops, then recover from dive.
fields.explanation:
POH section 3.1 page 28 contains the approved language for spin recovery.

A. Full forward stick is not necessary and may result in disorienting loss of control.

B. This is the exact language from the POH for spin recovery.

C. Stick full aft may delay recovery.

D. Full rudder against direction of rotation may be necessary to recover from spin.

fields.question_text: To bail out of the Discus CS:
fields.option_a: Unlock canopy with normal left-side lever forward, release right hinge side with red knob forward, throw off canopy, release harness, exit cockpit, pull parachute ripcord when clear.
fields.option_b: Release harness, unlock canopy with normal left-side canopy forward, release right hinge side with red knob forward, throw off canopy, exit cockpit, pull ripcord.
fields.option_c: Release harness, release right hinge side of canopy with red knob forward, unlock canopy from left side, throw off canopy, exit cockpit, pull ripcord.
fields.option_d: Unlock canopy with normal left-side lever forward, release right hinge side with red knob forward, throw off canopy, release harness, exit cockpit, allow static line tether to deploy parachute.
fields.explanation:
POH section 3.2 page 29 contains procedures for emergency exit.

A. This is the procedure for emergency exit, plus releasing the harness after the canopy has been jettisoned, then pulling the parachute ripcord when clear of the aircraft.

B. It is not recommended to release the harness before jettisoning the canopy since you need to be secured in order to operate the release mechanisms.

C. Same as B plus the normal side should be opened before releasing the hinges.

D. Although there is a static line tether connection available on the glider, it is rarely used, may not have been attached, or may not have worked -- always pull the parachute ripcord when clear of the aircraft.

fields.question_text: The maximum allowable speed on aerotow in the Discus CS is:
fields.option_a: 62 knots
fields.option_b: 81 knots
fields.option_c: 97 knots
fields.option_d: 108 knots
fields.explanation:
POH section 4.3 page 35 provides aerotow limits and procedures.

A. 62 knots is the recommended approach speed at max allowable weight.

B. 81 knots is the winch launch limit.

C. 97 knots is the aerotow limit.

D. 108 knots is the maneuvering speed and rough air limit.

fields.question_text:
Reasons the Discus CS requires a separate checkout at Skyline Soaring for flying with water ballast include the following:

I. Tire pressure required for the main landing gear wheel increases at total weight above 794 pounds.
II. Handling characteristics and speeds to fly change when full ballast is carried, increasing weight by approximately 400 pounds.
III. If ballast tanks are not completely emptied and dried after flight, they can freeze on the ground making the glider no longer airworthy.
IV. SSC cockpit cards are based on flying without ballast, and do not cover flight restrictions or weight and balance with ballast.
V. Flight with water ballast requires careful monitoring of inflight temps to avoid freezing of water in ballast tanks.
VI. Takeoff and landing procedures are different with water ballast.
VII. Internal baffling in the wings can hold water assymetrically, making takeoff difficult if not balanced.
VIII. Water can transfer from one wing to the other making takeoff difficult if not balanced.
IX. Recovery from a spin may be difficult with water ballast, especially if not symmetrical.
X. Filling wing tanks with water under pressure from a hose can damage the wings.
fields.option_a: All of the above except I, VI, and IX.
fields.option_b: All of the above except VIII and X.
fields.option_c: All of the above except VIII.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation:
All of the above restrictions except items IV and VIII are described in the POH.  

IV - The SSC cockpit cards, in the interest of keeping things simple, do not address flight with ballast, so this is still True.

VIII - The wing ballast tanks are not interconnected, though water can move to low side within each tank if one wing is allowed to stay low when partially filled, and will not redistrute immediately due to internal baffles when wings are levelled just before takeoff.  False.

fields.question_text: The maximum proven crosswind component for takeoff and landing in the Discus CS is:
fields.option_a: Not specified.
fields.option_b: 11 knots.
fields.option_c: 11 mph.
fields.option_d: 15 knots.
fields.explanation: POH section 2.12 page 27 specifies that the maximum crosswind component proven for takeoff and landing is 20 km/h (11 knots).

fields.question_text: During preflight of the Discus CS, check the following:
fields.option_a: Check that the main spar pin is fully home and secured.
fields.option_b: Three sets of static ports are clear (nose, under wing, and on boom near the tail).
fields.option_c: No unusual play when shaking the trailing edge of ailerons, rudder and elevator.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation:
A. Location and installation of the pin is covered in the POH, page 53. 

B. Location of static ports is described in separate items under daily inspection, POH item 4b page 33 and item 9 page 34.

C. POH daily inspection item 2c page 32 and item 6b page 33.

D. All of the above are in the POH.

fields.question_text: For a normal takeoff in the Discus CS without water ballast:
fields.option_a: Set trim to one third travel from full forward, stick neutral with intermediate to forward CG position, when elevator is effective raise the tail to level attitude.
fields.option_b: Set trim to one third travel from full forward, stick slightly forward if CG position is slightly aft, when elevator is effective raise tail to level attitude.
fields.option_c: Set trim to neutral, keep stick aft and tail on the ground until liftoff.
fields.option_d: A and B above.
fields.explanation:
POH section 4.3.1 page 35 describes airotow takeoff procedures.

A. Correct for intermediate to forward CG location.

B. Correct for aft CG position.

C. Not part of the recommendation, and would result in premature liftoff with a strong initial climbing vector.

D. Both A and B are covered in the POH recommendation.

fields.question_text: In high-speed flight in the Discus CS:
fields.option_a: Full deflection of controls may only be used up to Va (108 knots), only one third deflection at Vne.
fields.option_b: Full deflection of controls may be used at any speed.
fields.option_c: Airbrakes should never be used above Va (108 knots).
fields.option_d: Dive angle should not exceed 60 degrees.
fields.explanation:
POH section 4.6 page 43 describes high speed flight considerations:

A. Is correct, full deflection of controls is permitted up to Va/Vno, one third at Vne.

B. Is not correct, full deflection should not be used at Vne, especially the elevator.

C. Airbrakes may be used up to Vne, with caution.

D. Dive angle should not exceed 45 degrees without water ballast, and 30 degrees with ballast.

fields.question_text: For normal landings in the Discus CS:
fields.option_a: Touchdown should be at minimum speed, with airbrakes extended, approximately 38 knots.
fields.option_b: At minimum speed with proper flare just above the runway, touchdown is tail first.
fields.option_c: Flaring high can result in a stall and dropped-in landing with damage to the tail wheel and boom.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation:
POH section 4.11 page 52 describes approach and landing.

A. Is correct per POH

B. Is correct per POH

C. Is always correct for any glider.

D. All of the above are true.

fields.question_text: If it is necessary to add ballast to reach minimum allowable seat weight of 154 pounds (70 kg) including parachute:
fields.option_a: Lead or a sand/shot cushion must be securely held in place by attaching it to the lap belt brackets.
fields.option_b: A shot bag may be placed under the seat cushion, but does not need to be attached to the lap belt brackets.
fields.option_c: Wing ballast may be added to ensure the CG is within limits.
fields.option_d: Tail ballast may be added to ensure the CG is within limits.
fields.explanation:
A. POH section 2.7 page 21 requires ballast to be attached to the lap belt brackets, or bolted into the fuselage nose cone.

B. Simply putting weight under the seat cushion does not meet the POH requirement.

C. Wing ballast does not affect the POH minimum seat weight restriction.

D. Tail ballast does not affect the POH minimum seat weight restriction.
