fields.question_text: Flying at or below Manuevering Speed (Va) always protects from an over-G condition for which conditions:
fields.option_a: Elevator and half aileron
fields.option_b: Elevator only with spoilers closed
fields.option_c: Elevator and half rudder
fields.option_d: Elevator only, with spoilers closed or open
fields.explanation:
An aircraft's G-limit is reduced when ailerons are deflected due to extra forces they apply to the wings. Typically the G-limit with full aileron deflection is about 2/3 of the level-flight G-limit. Because Manueuvering Speed is defined as stall speed times the square of the G-limit, aileron deflection reduces Maneuvering Speed. 

Further, and separately, rudder deflection during high G-loading can create twisting forces on the tail boom that exceed the design limit. The combination of a high roll rate with rudder deflection severely reduces the G-limits on gliders.

Further, and separately, spoiler deployment blanks out part of the fattest wing area, which transfers loads farther out on the wing, increasing bending moments.

fields.question_text: Which of these aerobatic maneuvers are NOT approved for SSC ASK-21 gliders:
fields.option_a: Cuban Eight, Cloverleaf, and Barrel Roll
fields.option_b: Tail Slide, Outside Loop, and Snap Roll
fields.option_c: Split-S and Immelmann
fields.option_d: Chandelle and Lazy Eight
fields.explanation: Section 2.3. Prohibited Maneuvers in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide lists all prohibited maneuvers in option B.

fields.question_text: Parachutes must be worn while performing aerobatics maneuvers and each parachute must have received a certified repack within the past:
fields.option_a: 90 days
fields.option_b: Calendar year
fields.option_c: 180 days
fields.option_d: Two years
fields.explanation: 14 CFR 91.307 – PARACHUTES. Must be repacked within the previous 180 days. Each occupant must wear parachute if the flight will exceed a bank angle of 60 degrees relative to horizon or exceed a pitch attitude above or below 30 degrees relative to the horizon.

fields.question_text: At the maximum permissible speed of the ASK-21 (151 knots), what is the maximum permissible deflection of any flight control:
fields.option_a: Normal limits
fields.option_b: 2/3 of full deflection
fields.option_c: 1/2 of full deflection
fields.option_d: 1/3 of full deflection
fields.explanation: Page 9 of the ASK-21 POH states that at max permissible airspeed, only 1/3 of possible control deflections are permitted.

fields.question_text: What are the G-limits of the ASK-21 glider:
fields.option_a: +6.5 G to -4.0 G up to maneuvering speed
fields.option_b: +5.3 G to -3.0 G above maneuvering speed
fields.option_c: +3.5 G to -0.0 G with spoilers open
fields.option_d: All three.
fields.explanation:
See page 9 of the ASK-21 POH. 

These limits are for symmetrical maneuvers.

fields.question_text: Which statement is true for the Slow Roll in an ASK-21:
fields.option_a: It is super easy, just hold full aileron until you're right side up again.
fields.option_b: It is a low-stress maneuver that isn't even real aerobatics.
fields.option_c: It is very challenging and should be learned as two separate half-roll maneuvers.
fields.option_d: The ASK-21 glider just loves to roll inverted.
fields.explanation:
Section 3.5. Slow Roll in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide says:

A full 360-degree Slow Roll is a challenging maneuver in the ASK-21. Being comfortable with this maneuver—and knowing how to do it right—can save you someday if you experience a severe, unexpected inflight upset. While the Slow Roll is used in some manner in all advanced aerobatic maneuvers, it’s an excellent technique for recovering from severe inflight upsets.

It is highly recommended that you initially practice the Slow Roll as two separate maneuvers:

  1. First Half: Roll inverted and hold for a few seconds as you note where the horizon is during inverted flight. You will need this sight picture during the more advanced maneuvers. This also trains your muscle memory to apply forward stick pressure as you pass through inverted to prevent an inverted high-speed situation.
  2. Second Half: After a brief pause, continue in the same direction to roll out of inverted.

fields.question_text: The following additional equipment is required in the ASK-21 glider when performing aerobatics:
fields.option_a: Parachutes, G-meter in front seat, and barf bags
fields.option_b: Parachutes and G-meters in both cockpits.
fields.option_c: Parachutes, 5-point should harness, and rudder pedal foot loops
fields.option_d: Parachutes and 5-point should harness for loops only
fields.explanation: See page 8 in the ASK-21 POH, II.3 MINIMUM EQUIPMENT

fields.question_text: If you accidentally exceed the maximum permissible airspeed of 151 knots:
fields.option_a: Don't worry about it if you exceeded it briefly and by no more than 10 knots.
fields.option_b: Perform a structural damage/controllability check
fields.option_c: Ground the aircraft until it can be inspected by a certified mechanic
fields.option_d: Both B & C
fields.explanation:
Section 2.4. Inflight Procedures of the SSC Advanced Flying Guide says:

Any time an overspeed or over-G occurs, notify the DO, perform a structural damage/controllability check, and return immediately to land.

Ground the aircraft until it can be inspected by a certified mechanic.

fields.question_text: If you over-G the ASK-21 while performing aerobatics:
fields.option_a: Perform a structural damage/controllability check
fields.option_b: Return to land immediately
fields.option_c: Notify the DO and ground the aircraft
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation:
Section 2.4. Inflight Procedures of the SSC Advanced Flying Guide says:

Any time an overspeed or over-G occurs, notify the DO, perform a structural damage/controllability check, and return immediately to land.

Ground the aircraft until it can be inspected by a certified mechanic.

fields.question_text: When flying aerobatics, you can experience low or even negative G-loads, which could cause your arms and feet to float. Under these conditions, you should:
fields.option_a: Never remove your hands or feet from the flight controls while flying aerobatics.
fields.option_b: Just float along and enjoy the weird sensation.
fields.option_c: See if you can take a drink while upside down.
fields.option_d: Be sure to snap a picture while inverted. It will impress your friends.
fields.explanation:
Page 36 of the ASK-21 POH includes the following:

ATTENTION: Never release stick and rudder pedals when flying aerobatics.

fields.question_text: The recommend entry speed and pull for a Loop in the ASK-21 is:
fields.option_a: 108 knots, 3-4 Gs
fields.option_b: 79 knots, 3 Gs
fields.option_c: 92 knots, 2-3 Gs
fields.option_d: Whatever works best for you.
fields.explanation: See page 38 of the ASK-21 POH for a list of all entry conditions.

fields.question_text: When performing a Slow Roll, the following entry airspeed and pull are used:
fields.option_a: 89 knots, but no pull for a slow roll, just roll.
fields.option_b: 89 knots, with a 2 G pull to level flight before rolling.
fields.option_c: 89 knots, with a 2 G pull to 10-20° nose up before rolling.
fields.option_d: You can perfom one at any airspeed.
fields.explanation: See page 38 of the ASK-21 POH for a list of the entry speed and section 3.5. Slow Roll in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide for conditions.

fields.question_text: When performing a Loop, which statement(s) are true about the airspeed you should have as you go over the top:
fields.option_a: If speed exceeds 70 knots, abort the Loop and perform unusual attitude recovery.
fields.option_b: Speed over the top should be exactly half of your entry speed.
fields.option_c: Ideally you should be at 40-50 knots.
fields.option_d: Both A & C
fields.explanation: See Section 3.6. Loop in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text: The Lazy Eight is not really an aerobatic maneuver, so you don't need to wear a parachute.
fields.option_a: True, if bank doesn't exceed 60 and pitch doesn't exceed 30
fields.option_b: False, because the ideal target pitch attitude is 45 degrees nose up.
fields.option_c: False, becaue it's just too easy.
fields.option_d: Both A & B
fields.explanation: See Section 4.8. Lazy Eight in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text: How many sorties are recommended for you first day of flying glider aerobatics:
fields.option_a: No limit because glider aerobatics aren't real aerobatics.
fields.option_b: No more than two sorties your first day, even if you've flown aerobatics before.
fields.option_c: Glider aerobatics are disorienting, but only a wuss would impose limits.
fields.option_d: No more than four sorties your first day, unless you're really tough.
fields.explanation:
Glider aerobatics are disorienting even for experience aerobatics and military pilots.

Read Chapter 5 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text: How many aerobatics flights will I need before I can take up my friends and show them how cool I am?
fields.option_a: Most people can qualify for solo aerobatics within 10 aerobatics flights.
fields.option_b: There are different qualification standards for the various maneuvers.
fields.option_c: The SSC does not permit aerobatics to be flown wihout an aerobatics instructor.
fields.option_d: Only a few will be certified to fly aerobatics with friends and family.
fields.explanation: See Section 1.3. Sailplane Aerobatics in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text: What are the weather limits for flying aerobatics sorties at the Skyline Soaring Club?
fields.option_a: Standard VFR weather minimums apply.
fields.option_b: Visibility 5 statute miles with a ceiling of no lower than 5,000 feet.
fields.option_c: Visibility 3 statute miles with a ceiling of no lower than 5,000 feet.
fields.option_d: Visibility 10 statute miles with a ceiling of no lower than 5,000 feet.
fields.explanation: See Section 2.1. Preflight Procedures in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text: The following statement is true about the pitot extension tube for the ASK-21.
fields.option_a: Without one, there are different limiting speeds for aerobatics.
fields.option_b: Trick question, there is no such thing.
fields.option_c: It's nice to have, but doesn't affect any critical airspeed limitations.
fields.option_d: Flight without the extension is expressely prohibited by the POH.
fields.explanation:
See the Skyline Soaring Club Aerobatics Checklist. 

See pages 36 and 37 in the ASK-21 POH.

fields.question_text: On average, how much altitude will the ASK-21 lose during over-the-top maneuvers such as a Loop, or each leaf of a Cloverleaf, or each half of a Cuban 8:
fields.option_a: Because we pull up first, all over-the-top maneuvers leave us higher than we began.
fields.option_b: Each time over the top costs us about 300 feet of altitude loss.
fields.option_c: Each time over the top costs us about 100 feet of altitude loss.
fields.option_d: Each time over the top costs us about 200 feet of altitude loss.
fields.explanation: See the SSC Aerobatics Cockpit Card and read the NOTE at the end of each aerobatic maneuver for altitude changes on all maneuvers.

fields.question_text: The flying of aerobatics sorties by pilots of the Skyline Soaring Club requires the following:
fields.option_a: An aerobatics instructor must be with you on all sorties.
fields.option_b: Approval by the DO and maintaining radio contact on 123.0.
fields.option_c: The DO must open the SSC pre-approved aerobatics box.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation: See Section 2.1. Preflight Procedures in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text:
The emergency bailout procedures for the ASK-21 are found in the POH in the following section and pages.

Further, that section says that if circumstances allow, the front pilot should allow the rear pilot to bail out _________ (first? or second?).
fields.option_a: Section IV,9 on pages 35-36. First.
fields.option_b: Section IV.9 on pages 35-36, Second.
fields.option_c: Section VI.4 on pages 51-52, Second.
fields.option_d: Section III.2 on pages 22-23, First.
fields.explanation: Do you really need this one explained now that you found it in Section III.2 on pages 22-23!

fields.question_text:
The SSC Advanced Flying Guide contains many sections of text that are highlisted in red, bolded, and set off from the normal text.

Each of these red and bolded paragraphs are a WARNING that represent operating procedures or practices:
fields.option_a: . . which if not strictly observed could result in serious damage to the aircraft.
fields.option_b: . . that is essential to emphasize.
fields.option_c: . . that could result in personal injury or loss of life or loss of the aircraft if not carefully followed.
fields.option_d: None of the above! Surely, there is nothing at all hazardous about flying aerobatics.
fields.explanation:
While flying aerobatics is quite safe when done correctly and with respect for all that's happening, it certainly is hazardous when done without careful planning, study, and attention to detail. 

See Section 1.2. Overview of This Program in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide.

fields.question_text:
The maneuvers illustrated in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide must be flown only inside the club's approved aerobatics box.

This box comes with a set of waivers granted to us by the FAA, which were necessary to obtain and to adhere to because:
fields.option_a: We don't want to scare our airport neighbors.
fields.option_b: Aerobatics cannot be performed with 4nm of a Victor airway and one lies directly over us.
fields.option_c: It gives us a nice safe space that no one else is allowed to fly through.
fields.option_d: It has good viewing points for crowds on the ground.
fields.explanation:
Read all of Attachment 1 of the SSC Advanced Flying Guide and the Aerobatics Checklist for detailed depictions of the aerobatic box, the use of which is required by Federal Air Regulations for all of our club's aerobatic flying. 

Our field and flying area lies within 4nm of the centerlines of two Victor airways. We cannot perform aerobatics without a waiver from the FAA, which we have secured.

The top of our box gives us 500 feet of normal VFR separation from IFR traffic using those airways. 

A special mention about fake answer C: Our aerobatics box provides exactly ZERO protection from other aircraft flying through it any time. We do expect that club members will avoid the box when it is in use, but the box is not depicted on any aeronautical chart, and no pilots outside of the SSC will know of its activation. There will be NOTAM and no TFR. 

Constant traffic vigiliance and clearing turns before every maneuver are absolutely MUSTS!

fields.question_text: Gloders are not required to have transponders or ADS-B devices. The SSC has voluntarily elected to provide both of those devices in all of the club's aircraft. Legally, however, if either of those devices are inoperative, the aircraft could still be flown normally. Is this true for SSC gliders while performing aerobatics?
fields.option_a: No, Our aerobatics box waiver requires any glider using it to have a transponder set to 1202.
fields.option_b: Yes. Even doing aerobatics, we're still a glider with the same exemption.
fields.option_c: No. Our aerobatics box waiver required any glider using it to have a working ADS-B Out.
fields.option_d: Both A & C.
fields.explanation:
Read all of Attachment 1 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide for details on the restrictions to which we agreed in exchange for getting the waivers we need for the club's aerobatics box. 

The current SSC aerobatics box is expanded considerably compared to our previous box, which will greatly improve our ability to perform the aerobatic maneuvers properly. The restrictions to which we agreed will help other aircraft see and avoid our gliders while aerobatics are being performed, which helped us get approval for the larger box and the higher altitude.

fields.question_text: When assembling the PW-5, which wing should you insert first?
fields.option_a: The left wing.  All gliders have adopted this as standard.
fields.option_b: The right wing, because the manual said so.
fields.option_c: Either wing.  The glider is symmetrical.
fields.option_d: Panic and call Keith.
fields.explanation:
The Sailplane Maintenance Manual p. 3-8 (pdf p. 49), ¶ C.3. instructs: "rig the right-hand wing first...."

You may then insert the main bolts halfway to help hold the right wing in place while inserting the left wing.

fields.question_text: What is the maximum allowed gross weight of the PW-5?
fields.option_a: 300 lbs.
fields.option_c: 110 daN.
fields.explanation: The POH lists 300 kg (661.4 lbs) as the maximum weight of the aircraft. POH p. 2.4 (pdf p. 13), ¶ 2.4.

fields.question_text: During your preflight, you open the inspection plate to check the connections to control systems. After you close the inspection plate, should it be taped in place?
fields.option_a: No, wing tape is expensive and should not be wasted on non-control surfaces.
fields.option_b: No, we paid good money for the latch on the inspection plate, and should maximize that investment.
fields.option_c: No, the wing tape is usually kept in the hangar and it is a pain to go get it.
fields.option_d: Yes, the inspection plate must always be taped so we don't lose it.
fields.explanation: The inspection plate latch is insufficient to hold the inspection plate in place at flying speed, so the inspection plate must be taped in place.

fields.question_text: What is the Never Exceed speed (Vne) of the PW-5?
fields.option_a: 65 kts.
fields.option_b: 81 kts.
fields.option_c: 115 kts.
fields.option_d: 121 kts.
fields.explanation:
A. 65 kts is incorrect because that is the maximum winch launch (Vw) speed.

B. 81 kts is incorrect because that is the maneuvering (Va) speed and maximum aerotow (Vt) speed. 

C. 115 kts was the original limit per POH page 2.2 , but has been superseded by technical notes from the manufacturer and EASA

D. 121 kts is now correct after the manufacturer and EASA type certification notices.

fields.question_text: Someone gave you a hint to search online for the phrase "Smyk PW-5 Service Bulletin BS-17-08-19 dated February 21, 2008" and after finding it, you learned that the maximum operating altitude of the SSC's PW-5 is:
fields.explanation:
A. 16,404' MSL is incorrect because the original 5,000 m limitation in the POH was increased by a subsequent service bulletin.

B. 16,404' AGL is incorrect because it is a distractor based upon the obsolete 5,000 m limitation in the POH.

C. 18,000' MSL is incorrect because although it is the floor of Class A airspace, operations in Class A airspace are permitted under certain circumstances.

D. 36,089' MSL is correct because the maximum altitude was increased to 11,000 m by Service Bulletin BS-17-08-09 dated February 21, 2008.

fields.question_text: Assuming the gross weight of the glider does not provide for a more restrictive limitation, what is the maximum weight of the pilot, parachute or seat cushions, and baggage?
fields.option_a: 213 lbs
fields.option_b: 242 lbs
fields.option_d: There is no separate cockpit load limit; the gross weight controls.
fields.explanation:
A. 213 lbs is incorrect because that is the Sprite's maximum pilot weight.

B. 242 lbs is correct. The design maximum cockpit load is listed as 110 kg (242 lbs) in the POH at p. 2.7 (pdf p. 16), ¶ 2.12.  However, the max allowable gross weight may restrict this further.

C. 247.5 lbs is incorrect because the maximum cockpit load is 110 kg and not 110 daN.

D. is incorrect because there is a separate cockpit load limit published.

fields.question_text: The maximum strength of rope or weak-link is ____ according to the POH, and ____ according to the FAA.
fields.option_a: 700 lbs, 600 kg, because the POH specifies 700 and the regulation specifies 2x gross weight.
fields.option_b: 1574 lbs, 1323 lbs, because the POH specifies 700 daN, and the regulation specifies 2x gross weight
fields.option_c: 1374 lbs, 1374 lbs, because the POH supersedes the regulation in this case.
fields.option_d: 1238 lbs, 1238 lbs, because the limiting factor is the towplane's tost release.
fields.explanation:
The POH specifies 700 daN (1574 lbs) at p. 2.6 (pdf p. 15), ¶ 2.10.

14 CFR § 91.309(a)(3) requires a breaking strength no more than twice the gross weight of the aircraft (2 * 300 kg = 600 kg = 1323 lbs).

A. is incorrect because the POH specifies 700 daN, not 700 lbs.

B. is correct.

C. is incorrect because you are required to comply with both the regulation and the operating limitations in the POH.

D. is incorrect because 1238 lbs is the actual rated rope strength of SSC's polypropylene ropes, and not the limit of the towplane's tost hook.

fields.question_text: In the PW-5, the green arc on the airspeed indicator is from 38 KIAS to 81 KIAS.  38 KIAS is 1.1 times the stall speed for a heavy aircraft.  81 KIAS is:
fields.option_a: The maximum rough air speed
fields.option_b: The maneuvering speed
fields.option_c: Maximum aerotowing speed
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation: Pages 2.2 lists the airspeed limitations and page 2.3 list the airspeed indicator markings.  In this case, Vra (Vno) and Va and Vt are all the same, at 81 KIAS.

fields.question_text: When landing the PW-5, touchdown attitude should be:
fields.option_a: Tail wheel first
fields.option_b: Tail wheel and main gear simultaneously
fields.option_c: Main gear only (slightly tail low)
fields.option_d: Main gear and nose wheel simultaneously
fields.explanation: POH page 4.10 specifies that on landing the touchdown should be only on the main wheel, avoiding tail skid impact.  This is different from the Discus (tail wheel first) or ASK-21 (tail and main roughly at the same time).  Touching down nose first is never a good idea.  Touching down tail first may cause severe tail boom vibration and damage.  Landing with full spoilers may increase the chance of touching down tail first.

fields.question_text: The PW-5 is capable of spinning.  The procedure for recovering from a spin in the PW-5 is:
fields.option_a: Full opposite rudder, ease stick forward until rotation stops, centralize rudder and recover from dive.
fields.option_b: Full opposite rudder, pause, ease stick forward until rotation stop, centralize rudder and recover from dive.
fields.option_c: Ailerons neutral, full opposite rudder, ease stick forward until rotation stops, centralize rudder and recover from dive.
fields.option_d: Full opposite rudder, stick full forward until rotation stops, centralize rudder and recover from dive.
fields.explanation: POH page 3.2 specifies the steps for spin recovery. (A) are the steps for recovery in the Discus, (B) are the steps for recovery in the ASK-21, (C) are the steps explicitly listed for the PW-5.  (D) is probably not a good idea in any glider.  Subtle differences between A, B, C.  In practice, ailerons should always be neutral during recovery for most gliders.

fields.question_text: In the PW-5, the POH-recommended aerotow speed while climbing is:
fields.option_a: 60 KIAS
fields.option_b: 65-70 KIAS
fields.option_c: 50-75 KIAS
fields.option_d: 60 MPH
fields.explanation: POH page 4.9 specifies the recommended aerotow speed of 60 KIAS in a climb (A).  Option (B) may be commonly used by towplanes, but not specified in the POH.  (C) is the recommended range from the ASK-21 POH, and (D) is wrong because the PW-5 airspeeds are specified in KIAS.

fields.question_text: In the PW-5, before takeoff adjust the trim setting to:
fields.option_a: Neutral
fields.option_b: Position 1-2 for light pilots, and up to 5-6 for heavy pilots
fields.option_c: Full forward
fields.option_d: Full aft
fields.explanation: POH page 4.9 specifies setting 1-2 for light pilots and up to 5-6 for heavy pilots.

fields.question_text: In the PW-5, aerotow in the low tow position (including boxing the wake) is not recommended because:
fields.option_a: The glider is unstable in low tow position
fields.option_b: The cable rubs the front of the fuselage
fields.option_c: Sufficient elevator authority is not available
fields.option_d: Boxing the wake is never permitted solo
fields.explanation: POH page 4.9 recommends against flying under the towing airplane downwash because the cable rubs the fuselage.

fields.question_text: In the PW5, there are canopy locking hand grips on both sides.  Which of the following is FALSE?
fields.option_a: Pull back on the canopy grips just until you meet resistance
fields.option_b: Pull back on the canopy grips until the sliding rod by your shoulders no longer shows bare metal
fields.option_c: If the canopy does not align properly, you may need to pull the aft sides of the canopy rail inward by grasping the locking rod, or ask for help from somebody outside
fields.option_d: If the canopy rail goes wide to the outside, it is possible for the handle to be aft but not locked
fields.explanation: The canopy rarely seats completely without help to push or pull the lower aft corners inward.

fields.question_text: The red T-handle at the top of the instrument panel is:
fields.option_a: The tow cable release knob
fields.option_b: The rudder pedal adjust knob
fields.option_c: The canopy jettison knob
fields.option_d: The cabin air vent adjust knob
fields.explanation: POH page 7.2 describes the location of the emergency canopy release.  Without a parachute, there are few cases where you would ever want to touch this.

fields.question_text: In the PW5, the wheel brake is activated by:
fields.option_a: Pulling full aft on the spoiler handle
fields.option_b: Squeezing the brake handle on the lower part of the control stick
fields.option_c: Squeezing the brake handle on the front of the spoiler handle
fields.option_d: Pulling up on the brake lever just behind the trim adjuster
fields.explanation: POH page 7.2 identifies the brake lever.  A is how the ASK-21 works, B is how the Discus works, D is a Blanik feature.  Do NOT land with the brake lever on the spoiler handle already squeezed.

fields.question_text: Refer to the cockpit cards with cockpit loading chart based on the 2 Jan 2022 weight and balance.  You weigh 230 pounds as dressed for your flight, and intend to position the seat back full aft.  Are you legal to fly N505CC?
fields.option_a: Yes, because the allowable cockpit load is 110 kg or 242 pounds.
fields.option_b: Yes, because the CG position would be 52% of allowable aft range.
fields.option_c: No, because the max allowable gross weight of the glider limits pilot weight to 223.5 pounds.
fields.option_d: Yes, because it is permissible to exceed max allowable gross weight if not flown for aerobatics.
fields.explanation:
C is correct, because the most recent empty weight of 437 pounds limits the cockpit load to 223.5 pounds.

A is not correct, because the design cockpit load is still subject to the MAGW limit.

B is not correct, because the allowable CG position is still subject to the MAGW limit.

D is not correct, because there is no concession here for exceeding the MAGL limit.

fields.question_text: Refer to the cockpit cards with cockpit loading chart based on the 2 Jan 2022 weight and balance.  You weigh 145 pounds as dressed for your flight, and intend to position the seat back full forward.  Are you legal to fly this PW5?
fields.option_a: No, because the minimum pilot weight for all gliders is 70 kg or 154 pounds.
fields.option_b: No, because the CG limit would be too far aft.
fields.option_c: Yes, the weight is within the allowable range and the CG would be at 90% of allowable aft range.
fields.option_d: Yes, but only if you sit on a 25 pound shot bag.
fields.explanation:
C is correct, the weight is within cockpit limits (121 to 242 pounds) and the CG is within limits but right at the club-recommended 90% aft range.

A is not correct, for this glider the design cockpit minimum was 55 kg or 121 pounds, with the restriction that if below 60 kg or 132 pounds the seat must be full forward.

B is not correct, the CG is right at the club recommended aft limit at 90%.

D is not correct, a shot bag is not required, but if you did, 25 pounds would move the CG forward to about 74%, which might be more comfortable to handle.

fields.question_text: Section 4.5.7 of the POH says that "looping" aerobatics are approved in the PW-5. Are you excited about flying some loops?
fields.option_a: Yes, but only with a parachute
fields.option_b: Yes, if I can squeeze a CFIG in with me
fields.option_c: No, due to SSC ops manual prohibitions
fields.option_d: No, because I'm scared of loops, but I'll certainly try some if I get brave enough
fields.explanation: Section 3.10 Aerobatics in the SSC Ops manual prohibits aerobatics except when flying dual and even then with all other requirements satisfied.

fields.question_text: The PW-5 seems to like "mean pilots," but has a special note that applies to some "light pilots."
fields.option_a: Even though the PW-5 has no limits on "mean pilots," they should get an attitude adjustment.
fields.option_b: The statement is wrong, there are no special notes about "light pilots"
fields.option_c: A very bright "light pilot" is allowed to fly at night
fields.option_d: A "light pilot" who weighs less than 60kg must set the back-rest at the front limit position
fields.explanation: The PW-5 POH has a CAUTION in Section 6.2 that states: PILOTS OF THE BODY+PARACHUTE WEIGHT BELOW 60KG MUST HAVE THE BACK-REST LOCATED IN LIMIT FRONT POSITION.

fields.question_text: What does the PW-5 POH say about side slips?
fields.option_a: Any airspeed and bank angle are allowable.
fields.option_b: Minimum airspeed is 49 knots and max bank angle of 20 degrees to maintain heading
fields.option_c: Minimum airspeed is 49 knots, any bank angle, and the airspeed indication will always be valid
fields.option_d: Maximum airspeed is 49 knots, but the airspeed indication will not be valid
fields.explanation: Section 4.5.3 FLIGHT says that side slips should be performed at an airspeed of 49 knots or higher and that heading can be maintained at up to 20 degrees of bank. It also says that the airspeed indicator will be invalid.

fields.question_text: The yellow arc on the airspeed indicator is from 81 to 115 knots with a redline at 115 because:
fields.option_a: Those markings show the correct limits.
fields.option_b: Those markings are incorrect because the max limit (Vne) has been increased to 121 knots
fields.option_c: The yellow arc shows the range for performing aerobatics
fields.option_d: The yellow arc and red line can't be wrong!
fields.explanation: 121 kts is the correct Vne after the manufacturer and EASA type certification notices, but the airspeed indicator markings have not been changed.

fields.question_text: In the SSC Advanced Flying Guide, a CAUTION signifies:
fields.option_a: Operating procedures or practices which, if not strictly observed, could result in damage to the aircraft.
fields.option_b: Operating procedures or practices that could result in personal injury or loss of life, or loss of the aircraft if not carefully followed.
fields.option_c: Operating procedures or practices which might make you barf.
fields.option_d: Operating procedures or practices which are very scary to perform.
fields.explanation: See Chapter 1 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide

fields.question_text: The build-up principle to learning aerobatics means:
fields.option_a: Building excitement for your friends to come to the airport to watch.
fields.option_b: To start out slowly and work on new techniques to build up to performing complete aerobatic maneuvers.
fields.option_c: To build up your courage before each aerobatics flight.
fields.option_d: To never fly aerobatics when there are cloud build-ups in the area.
fields.explanation: See Chapter 5 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide

fields.question_text: Which is these is true about G-LOC:
fields.option_a: It’s a device on the control stick that can lock out the possibility of exceeding the aircraft’s G limits.
fields.option_b: It’s a loss of consciousness occurring from high g-forces draining blood away from the brain, causing cerebral hypoxia.
fields.option_c: You can minimize it by performing the AGSM.
fields.option_d: Both B and C are correct.
fields.explanation: See Chapter 5 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide

fields.question_text: Club aerobatics are restricted to the designated Aerobatics Practice Area (APA):
fields.option_a: To ensure separation from all other air traffic.
fields.option_b: To ensure separation from other club glider traffic.
fields.option_c: Because aerobatics in our area violate some FARs, which are waived when we fly inside our activated APA.
fields.option_d: So that club members on the ground can watch and make videos of your aerobatics.
fields.explanation: See 14 CFR 91.303(d) and (e)

fields.question_text: Your tolerance to G loading:
fields.option_a: There's not much you can do. Some people can tolerate G’s, and some can’t.
fields.option_b: Can be enhanced by not working out hard the day before flying aerobatics.
fields.option_c: Can be enhanced by having good carbohydrate stores in your body.
fields.option_d: Both B and C are correct.
fields.explanation: See Chapter 5 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide, which quotes ad U.S. Air Force Web site for fighter pilots.

fields.question_text: In the SSC Advanced Flying Guide, a NOTE signifies:
fields.option_a: Operating procedures that will wow the crowd at an airshow.
fields.option_b: Operating procedure or a condition that is essential to emphasize.
fields.option_c: Operating procedures that don’t really matter.
fields.option_d: Operating procedures that are especially disorienting.
fields.explanation: See Chapter 1 in the SSC Advanced Flying Guide

fields.question_text: As part of the PW-5 preflight cockpit checks, confirm that the cable end connection is securely fastened at the pull-handle for the:
fields.option_a: Tow hook release
fields.option_b: Rudder pedal adjust
fields.option_c: Emergency canopy jettison
fields.option_d: All of the above
fields.explanation: Although not listed specifically in the POH, loose connections have been found on our PW-5 in the past.  Easy to check.

fields.question_text: The demonstrated crosswind performance of the PW-5 at takeoff and landing is:
fields.option_a: Not specified.
fields.option_b: 12 knots
fields.option_c: 12 meters/second
fields.option_d: 12 mph
fields.explanation: 5.3 of the flight manual states that the demonstrated crosswind performance of the PW-5 at takeoff and landing is 12 knots. It further states that "during the takeoff and landing ground run on two wheels the sailplane is practically non-sensitive to the crosswind action."

fields.question_text: Who has the final authority on the field as to the sequencing of launches?
fields.option_a: Chief Duty Officer
fields.option_b: Primary Duty Instructor
fields.option_c: Duty Officer
fields.option_d: Tow Pilot
fields.explanation: The ops manual says that the duty officer has the final authority about the launch order.

fields.question_text: During the operating day the ADO is approached by a newcomer seeking information about SSC.  What should the ADO do?
fields.option_a: Introduce yourself and describe the activities of the club.
fields.option_b: Politely refer the visitor to the DO who will describe the activities of the club.
fields.option_c: Politely refer the visitor to a club member other than the DO who will explain the activities of the club.
fields.option_d: Politely tell the visitor that as the ADO you must keep your full attention on the flight line and may not be distracted by conversations with visitors.
fields.explanation: The Duty officer is busy keeping the operations running smoothly.  There are plenty of members who would like to talk about the club.  Direct the visitor to one of those people instead.

fields.question_text: During the operating day at what time may the ADO be in a location or position where he cannot see both the runway and the pattern?
fields.option_a: When there is a lull in the operations and no gliders are staged for launch.
fields.option_b: Never
fields.option_c: When nature calls, with the permission of the DO
fields.option_d: When there are other members available to launch gliders and watch the pattern.
fields.explanation: The ADO has to be around the runway as much as possible, potty breaks notwithstanding.

fields.question_text: What is the highest wind speed at which hangar doors on both sides may be open simultaneously?
fields.option_a: 8 knots
fields.option_b: 10 knots
fields.option_c: 12 knots
fields.option_d: 14 knots
fields.explanation: If both hangar doors are open, the wind will whip through the hangars, causing aircraft to shift around. Club policy is to have one shut at 10 knots or above.

fields.question_text: At the beginning of the operating day prior to the DO briefing where should the gliders be positioned and what other action should be taken?
fields.option_a: Line the gliders side to side in the marked positions on the south side of the ramp.  Ensure that there is no overlap of the wings, close and lock the canopies, place shot bags on the upwind wing tips if the winds exceed 10 knots, open the spoilers if possible.
fields.option_b: Line the gliders side to side in the marked positions on the south side of the ramp.  Ensure that there is no overlap of the wings, close and lock the canopies, place shot bags on the downwind wing tips if the winds exceed 10 knots, open the spoilers if possible.
fields.option_c: Position the gliders in single file with tails pointed toward the taxiway in the sequence they will be launched. Place shot bags on the upwind wing tips if the winds exceed 10 knots.
fields.option_d: Position the gliders in single file with tails pointed toward the taxiway in the sequence they will be launched. Place shot bags on the downwind wing tips if the winds exceed 10 knots.
fields.explanation: It is really important to close AND LOCK the canopies.  Just having it closed isn't enough.  We have had gusts of wind strong enough to open the canopies that were closed. (Even on the ASK-21!)

fields.question_text: The club owns VHF airband handheld and base station radios.  With regard to these radios:
fields.option_a: The ADO should carry a handheld VHF airband radio tuned to 123.0 at all times and use it to monitor the Front Royal Unicom and flight operations.
fields.option_b: The ADO should rely on the audio from the Front Royal Unicom and base station radios to monitor communications on 123.0.
fields.option_c: The ADO should carry a handheld VHF airband radio tuned to 123.3 at all times and use it monitor communications with gliders.
fields.option_d: The ADO should rely on the audio from the Front Royal Unicom and base station radios to monitor communications with gliders on 123.3.
fields.explanation: It is really important to have the radio tuned to the correct frequency. The radio doesn't do you any good if it's not on your person and not tuned to the right frequency!

fields.question_text: When may a guest be present on the ramp?
fields.option_a: Never
fields.option_b: When accompanied by a Skyline member with permission of the DO.
fields.option_c: When accompanied by a Skyline member.
fields.option_d: After the guest is briefed by a Skyline member regarding operations and safety.
fields.explanation: We can't have random people milling about the launch line.  Have them escorted by a Skyline member. Don't bother the DO with this.

fields.question_text: When may a guest be present on the runway
fields.option_a: Never
fields.option_b: When accompanied by a Skyline member with permission of the DO
fields.option_c: When accompanied by a Skyline member.
fields.option_d: After the guest is briefed by a Skyline member regarding operations and safety.
fields.explanation: The runway is a critical location with many safety hazards.  The duty officer should be involved in who is on the runway.

fields.question_text: When may a guest be present on the taxiway
fields.option_a: Never
fields.option_b: When accompanied by a Skyline member with permission of the DO.
fields.option_c: When accompanied by a Skyline member.
fields.option_d: After the guest is briefed by a Skyline member regarding operation and safety.
fields.explanation: There are plenty of hazards on the taxiway.  The DO should be involved in allowing non-pilots to be escorted to the taxiway.

fields.question_text: What are the primary duties of the ADO while operations are underway?
fields.option_a: Serve as launch officer for all launches.
fields.option_b: Serve as launch officer or retrieve gliders depending upon circumstances.
fields.option_c: Serve as launch officer or retrieve gliders depending upon the circumstances and monitor the pattern.
fields.option_d: Serve as launch officer for all launches (unless the ADO has designated a temporary launch officer who is known to be qualified to serve as a wing runner) and monitor the pattern.
fields.explanation: Sometimes the ADO is needed elsewhere, at which point the DO can designate somebody to be the launch officer.

fields.question_text: May the ADO be assisted by other Skyline members?
fields.option_a: The ADO may seek assistance from other Skyline members present at the field to perform any of the ADO duties.
fields.option_b: The ADO should generally serve as launch officer for all (or almost all) launches and may seek assistance from other Skyline members in positioning gliders or the ramp and retrieving gliders with tow vehicles.
fields.option_c: The ADO should generally retrieve gliders that have landed and delegate to other Skyline members the duty to serve as launch officer.
fields.option_d: The ADO should generally serve as launch officer and should retrieve gliders that have landed and to tow them back to the ramp area.
fields.explanation: The ADO is supposed to be the primary person responsible for hooking up and launching gliders. The other tasks can be delegated by the ADO if conditions merit.

fields.question_text: When a glider is positioned for launch and before the tow rope is connected the launch officer should:
fields.option_a: Present the end of the tow rope to the glider pilot for inspection and approval.
fields.option_b: Ensure that the canopy is locked.
fields.option_c: Ensure that the tail dolly has been removed.
fields.option_d: All of the above.
fields.explanation: All of these are true statements.

fields.question_text: When the glider is positioned for launch and after the tow rope is connected, the launch officer should:
fields.option_a: Ask and confirm that the pilot is ready for launch.
fields.option_b: Move behind the spoiler of the wing.
fields.option_c: Signal the tow pilot to take up slack in the tow rope.
fields.option_d: All of the above, in the order stated

fields.question_text: In this scenario, the launch officer is running the left wing, and the glider is taking off with a left crosswind. As the launch begins and the glider starts to move down the runway, the launch officer should:
fields.option_a: Release the wing
fields.option_b: Run beside the left wing holding the wing level and give a forward shove on the wing as it is released
fields.option_c: Run beside the wing holding the wing level, releasing the wing without imparting any fore or aft force on the wing tip
fields.option_d: Run beside the wing holding the wing slightly lower than neutral, releasing the wing without imparting any fore or aft force on the wing tip
fields.explanation: https://www.faa.gov/regulations_policies/handbooks_manuals/aviation/glider_handbook/gfh_chapter_7.pdf Page 7-6, and figure 7-4

fields.question_text: As the tow plane is landing what should be the condition of the next glider to be launched and where should it be positioned?
fields.option_a: The pilot and passenger should be aboard and strapped in, the ADO should have confirmed that tow release and positive control checks have been performed, the radio is set to 123.0 and the pilot is ready for push out.  The glider main gear should be on the hash mark on the taxiway at the entrance to the ramp.
fields.option_b: The pilot and passenger should be aboard and strapped in, the ADO should have confirmed that tow release and positive control checks have been performed, the radio is set to 123.0 and the pilot is ready for launch.  The glider should be pushed onto the runway waiting for the tow plane.
fields.option_c: The pilot and passenger should be aboard and strapped in, the ADO should have confirmed that tow release and positive control checks have been performed, the radio is  4 set to 123.0 and the pilot is ready for push out.  The glider main gear should be on the hash mark on the taxiway at the entrance to the ramp.  As soon as the tow plane clears the runway the ADO should confirm that the pattern is clear and begin pushing to the hold short line. As soon as the ADO confirms that the glider pilot has made a radio call announcing the launch, the pattern is still clear and the tow plane is approaching the glider may be pushed onto the runway.
fields.option_d: a and c are each correct

fields.question_text: The glider has just been pushed past the hold short line and the tow plane is approaching on the taxiway.  The ADO hears a radio call from a glider entering the pattern and sees the glider beginning the downwind leg.  The ADO should:
fields.option_a: Hurry the launch sequence so that the launching glider will be out of the way before the glider in the pattern reaches the runway.
fields.option_b: Stop the push out and push the glider back past the hold short line.
fields.option_c: Stop the push out and push the glider back to the hash mark at the ramp entrance so that the glider is clear of the taxiway and the grass area on the north side of the runway.
fields.option_d: Continue the launch without hurrying the launch sequence to avoid causing undue pressure on the glider pilot as he prepares for launch.
fields.explanation: it is important to keep the runway clear if there is any inbound glider traffic.

fields.question_text: The glider has been pushed onto the runway and the tow plane is positioned in front of the glider ready to take up slack in the tow line.  The ADO hears a radio call from a glider entering the pattern and sees the glider beginning the downwind leg.
fields.option_a: Hurry the launch sequence so that the launching glider will be out of the way before the glider in the pattern reaches the runway.
fields.option_b: Stop the launch and push the glider back past the hold short line.
fields.option_c: Stop the launch and push the glider back to the hash mark at the entrance to the ramp so that the glider is clear of the grass area on the north side of the runway.
fields.option_d: Continue the launch without hurrying the launch sequence to avoid causing undue pressure on the glider pilot as he prepares for launch.
fields.explanation:
You should never rush the pilots into a launch! 

If you're already on the runway, it's unlikely you can get everything off in time before the glider is on final approach.

fields.question_text: The glider is being pushed out to the hold short line and the tow plane is proceeding up the taxiway.   The ADO hears a radio call from the tow plane indicating that the pilot is going to refuel the tow plane.  What should the ADO do?
fields.option_a: Stop at the hold short line and wait until the tow plane is refueled and begins to taxi before pushing out the glider onto the runway.
fields.option_b: Ensure that the glider pilot has made a radio call announcing the launch and then push the glider onto the runway in the launch position to await the tow plane.
fields.option_c: Stop at the hold short line and wait until the tow plane is refueled and begins to taxi before pushing the glider onto the runway.  Ask the pilot and any passengers to get out of the glider while waiting for the tow plane in case their help is needed to push the glider clear of the runway.
fields.option_d: Stop the push out and return the glider to the hash mark at the ramp entrance so that the glider is clear of the grass area north of the runway as well as the taxiway. When the tow plane is refueled and begins to taxi begin the push out if the pattern is clear.

fields.question_text: A glider is entering the downwind leg of the landing pattern.  There is no wind and the glider is flying a normal pattern.  Approximately how much time will elapse until the glider crosses the runway threshold?
fields.option_a: 30 seconds
fields.option_b: 90 seconds
fields.option_c: 180 seconds
fields.option_d: 2 minutes
fields.explanation: On average, it's 90 seconds from the moment the glider turns from the 45 to downwind.  60 seconds abeam the touchdown point on downwind, 30 seconds while on base.

fields.question_text: The ADO observes a condition that he believes makes continued operations unsafe.  He should:
fields.option_a: Notify the DO.
fields.option_b: Notify the Duty Instructor
fields.option_c: Notify the DO, Duty Instructor, and Tow Pilot
fields.option_d: Stop operations
fields.explanation: every member at the airport is empowered to shut down operations at any time, due to a safety issue.  Simply approach the duty officer, and say "We need a safety stand-down" and all operations will stop, and an emergency meeting will convene to discuss the issue.

fields.question_text: How does the ADO know that the battery chargers are connected and turned on?
fields.option_a: All power cords are connected.
fields.option_b: Handheld radios are seated in their chargers and charge lights are on, charge lights are on for chargers connected to the glider batteries, the on/off switch on the base station radio charger is illuminated and the charge controller green and red lights are illuminated.  The charge controller is inside the base station radio case above the battery.
fields.option_c: Everything looks good.
fields.option_d: The ADO hears the radios working
fields.explanation: "Everything looks good" is insufficient.

fields.question_text: A glider is in the pattern preparing to land on runway 28. There is no tow vehicle waiting to retrieve the glider. What should the ADO do?
fields.option_a: Drive one of the tow vehicles down the taxiway to the midfield turn-off.  Plan to arrive there before the glider stops rolling.  When the glider has stopped, quickly walk/run out to the glider to assist the pilot (and passenger) in exiting the glider and pushing it off the runway.
fields.option_b: Drive one of the tow vehicles down the taxiway to the midfield turn-off.  Plan to arrive there before the glider stops rolling.  When the pilot (and passenger) have pushed the glider to the intersection of the taxiway and midfield turn-off hook up the glider and tow it back to the ramp.
fields.option_c: Ask one of the Skyline members to drive one of the tow vehicles down the taxiway to the midfield turn-off.  Ask them to arrive there before the glider stops rolling.  When the glider has stopped they should quickly walk/run out to the glider to assist the pilot (and passenger) in exiting the glider and pushing it off the runway.
fields.option_d: Ask one of the Skyline members to drive one of the tow vehicles down the taxiway to the midfield turn-off.  When the pilot and passenger have pushed the glider to the intersection of the taxiway and midfield turn-off the tow driver should hook up the glider and tow it back to the ramp.

fields.question_text: What is the primary benefit of the 'Kiosk' user account on the club's duty laptop?
fields.option_a: It allows the duty officer to bypass the logsheet creation process
fields.option_b: It keeps the duty officer logged in without requiring manual credentials
fields.option_c: It provides full administrative access to site configurations
fields.option_d: it enables offline flight logging for the entire club membership
fields.explanation: The 'Club Laptop' Kiosk user is pre-authenticated to streamline operations and ensure duty officers don't have to log in or log off. The kiosk mode has to be set up by a club webmaster, and the kiosk mode stays logged in on that device for a year, before its token expires.

fields.question_text: A glider is airborne. Launch time is entered, but release altitude was left blank. What will happen if you attempt to finalize later without correcting it?
fields.option_a: It will auto-fill the most common altitude
fields.option_b: It will fail validation
fields.option_c: It will finalize with a warning
fields.option_d: It will assume 0 feet
fields.explanation: Release altitude must not be blank (----). Remember that "0" (zero) is a valid entry, blank is not). Otherwise, validation will fail.

fields.question_text: You attempt to finalize today's logsheet, after all the operations are done. After clicking "Finalize" you are redirected to the Finance page with a red banner. What is the most likely cause?
fields.option_a: Missing landing time
fields.option_b: Missing payment method
fields.option_c: Missing release altitude
fields.option_d: Towplane Hobbs times missing
fields.explanation: Financial validation failures redirect to Finance page. Flight validation failures stay on logsheet page. All of the other potential validation failures don't have anything to do with the payments or who is paying for these fees.  They're all missing information that are associated with the flight logs. It's best just to read the red banner and give the program what it wants for what's missing.

fields.question_text: A self-launch glider is flown. You indicate the tow plane is "Self-Launch", but you accidentally assign a tow pilot. What is the likely result?
fields.option_a: Finalization will fail
fields.option_b: It’s harmless
fields.option_c: Towplane closeout will require tach entries
fields.option_d: The flight converts to aerotow
fields.explanation: Assigning a real towplane/tow pilot creates validation requirements for closeout data.

fields.question_text: A glider is grounded due to a maintenance issue entered earlier. You attempt to log a new flight with it. What happens?
fields.option_a: It appears normally
fields.option_b: It appears but warns
fields.option_c: It does not appear in dropdown
fields.option_d: It blocks finalization later
fields.explanation: Grounded aircraft are removed from available selection.

fields.question_text: At the end of the day, you finalize without entering anything in Safety Issues because nothing notable happened. Is this correct?
fields.option_a: No, safety field is mandatory
fields.option_b: Yes, leave blank if no issues
fields.option_c: Must enter "No Issues"
fields.option_d: Admin must fill it
fields.explanation: Safety field should be left blank if no notable safety issues occurred.

fields.question_text: What is the Never Exceed Speed (VNE) for the PW-5 in smooth air?
fields.option_a: 119 knots (220 km/h)
fields.option_b: 151 knots (280 km/h)
fields.option_c: 135 knots (250 km/h)
fields.option_d: 108 knots (200 km/h)
fields.explanation: Answer: A. Per the Limitations section, the VNE is 220 km/h (119 kts). (Ref: Flight Manual Page 2.2)

fields.question_text: What is the Maneuvering Speed (VA) for the PW-5?
fields.option_a: 70 knots (130 km/h)
fields.option_b: 81 knots (150 km/h)
fields.option_c: 86 knots (160 km/h)
fields.option_d: 97 knots (180 km/h)
fields.explanation: Answer: C. The design maneuvering speed is 160 km/h (86 kts). (Ref: Flight Manual Page 2.2)

fields.question_text: Which of the following maneuvers is PROHIBITED in the PW-5?
fields.option_a: Loop
fields.option_b: Stall
fields.option_c: Inverted Flight
fields.option_d: Lazy Eight
fields.explanation: Answer: C. The PW-5 is certified in the Utility category; however, inverted flight and intentional spins are generally restricted or require specific supplemental approval. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 2.8)

fields.question_text: Up to what speed may the airbrakes be extended?
fields.option_a: Up to VNE (220 km/h)
fields.option_b: Only up to 150 km/h
fields.option_c: Up to VA (160 km/h)
fields.option_d: Only during the landing flare
fields.explanation: Answer: A. The airbrakes can be opened up to the maximum speed VNE. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 2.3)

fields.question_text: How does the PW-5 typically behave during a straight-ahead stall?
fields.option_a: Violent wing drop
fields.option_b: Sudden pitch up
fields.option_c: Gentle nose drop with vibrating control stick
fields.option_d: It cannot be stalled
fields.explanation: Answer: C. The PW-5 exhibits a gentle stall, usually preceded by a slight vibration of the stick and followed by a nose-down pitch. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 4.7)

fields.question_text: During rigging, the wing spar pins are secured by:
fields.option_a: Safety wire.
fields.option_b: Spring-loaded locking pins (Ball locks)
fields.option_c: Plastic tape only
fields.option_d: Large bolts and nuts
fields.explanation: Answer: B. The main pins are secured by self-locking spring pins. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 4.2)

fields.question_text: According to the manual, what should be checked regarding the airbrakes during the daily inspection?
fields.option_a: That they only open on the left wing
fields.option_b: Proper extension and simultaneous locking
fields.option_c: That they are oiled with heavy grease
fields.option_d: Nothing; they are self-inspecting
fields.explanation: Answer: B. Ensure both airbrakes extend fully and lock simultaneously in the closed position. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 4.5)

fields.question_text: How does rain affect the PW-5's performance?
fields.option_a: It improves the glide ratio
fields.option_b: It significantly increases the sink rate and stall speed
fields.option_c: It has no measurable effect
fields.option_d: It makes the glider more stable
fields.explanation: Answer: B. Rain or contamination (bugs) on the wing increases sink rate and stall speed by about 5-10 km/h. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 4.11)

fields.question_text: Which of the following is part of the "Minimum Equipment" for VFR flight?
fields.option_a: Transponder
fields.option_b: Airspeed indicator and Altimeter
fields.option_c: Two-way Radio
fields.option_d: GPS Navigation
fields.explanation: Answer: B. Basic VFR requires an airspeed indicator, altimeter, and a 4-point safety harness. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 2.9)

fields.question_text: According to the manual, what is the first action required for recovery from a developed spin?
fields.option_a: Push the stick full forward
fields.option_b: Apply full rudder opposite to the direction of the spin
fields.option_c: Check that the ailerons are neutral
fields.option_d: Open the airbrakes immediately
fields.explanation: Answer: C. Section 3.5 specifies that the pilot must first check that ailerons are neutral before applying opposite rudder. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 3.2)

fields.question_text: During a spiral dive recovery, what is the correct sequence of control inputs?
fields.option_a: Pull back on the stick, then level the wings with ailerons.
fields.option_b: Level the wings with coordinated aileron and rudder, then recover from the dive.
fields.option_c: Deploy full airbrakes and wait for the nose to rise
fields.option_d: Apply full opposite rudder and push the stick forward
fields.explanation: Answer: B. Recovery is achieved by canceling the bank with coordinated aileron/rudder first, then pulling out of the dive. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 3.2)

fields.question_text: How are the two main wing pins (spar pins) secured after they are pushed into the fuselage?
fields.option_a: By tightening a 19mm nut.
fields.option_b: By spring-loaded safety pins (ball-locks) on the pins themselves
fields.option_c: By safety wire through the pin heads
fields.option_d: By a secondary locking bolt located behind the pilot's head
fields.explanation: Answer: B. The main pins are secured by self-locking spring pins. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 4.2)

fields.question_text: When de-rigging the aircraft, which component should be removed FIRST?
fields.option_a: The left wing.
fields.option_b: The main spar pins.
fields.option_c: The horizontal stabilizer
fields.option_d: The pitot tube.
fields.explanation: <p class="explanation"><strong>Answer: C.</strong> The manual specifies that the horizontal stabilizer should be removed first during the de-rigging sequence. (Ref: Flight Manual Page 4.4)</p>
